### COR-b019d3b9-2 — deriveAuditState's top-level 'blocked' branch is unreachable — no obligation is ever assigned state 'blocked'

Every obligation pushed in deriveAuditState is built via obligation(id, state, reason) with state literally 'missing', 'stale', or 'satisfied' (via has()/staleOrSatisfied()/designReviewPassState() etc.) — none of the ~20 obligation() call sites in this function ever pass 'blocked'.

- Severity: medium
- Confidence: high
- Lens: correctness
- Grounding: grounded
- Files: `src/audit/orchestrator/state.ts:593–600`
- Details: Every obligation pushed in deriveAuditState is built via obligation(id, state, reason) with state literally 'missing', 'stale', or 'satisfied' (via has()/staleOrSatisfied()/designReviewPassState() etc.) — none of the ~20 obligation() call sites in this function ever pass 'blocked'. The subsequent `obligations.some((o) => o.state === 'blocked')` check can therefore never be true, so deriveAuditState can never itself derive AuditTopLevelStatus 'blocked' — that value only ever reaches the bundle via a separate direct construction (buildBlockedAuditState in envelope.ts, used by reviewRun.ts), not through this function's own obligation-driven logic.
- Evidence: 3 items (top: "src/audit/orchestrator/state.ts:597 - status = "blocked"; is guarded by a condition that scans `obligations`, the array this same function builds") — see audit-findings.json for the full list

### COR-2f47dae1 — derivePhaseCut does not colocate cyclic modules despite its own documented/emitted claim

derivePhaseCut's cycle handling returns 0 for a back-edge inside computeTier instead of grouping cycle members at one tier.

- Severity: medium
- Confidence: high
- Lens: correctness
- Grounding: grounded
- Files: `src/remediate/contractPipeline/phaseCut.ts:16–18`, `src/remediate/contractPipeline/phaseCut.ts:82–86`, `src/remediate/contractPipeline/phaseCut.ts:294`
- Details: derivePhaseCut's cycle handling returns 0 for a back-edge inside computeTier instead of grouping cycle members at one tier. Tracing the algorithm for a 2-node cycle A<->B (A sorts before B in the outer loop) gives tier(B)=1 and tier(A)=2 — two DIFFERENT tiers, not 'placed together' as the module's header doc and the PhaseCut.has_cycle doc comment both claim. renderPhaseCutSection then tells the design-critique reviewer 'its members were tiered together' even when, per this trace, they were not — the tier split is an artifact of module-name sort order / recursion order, not a principled grouping.
- Evidence: 4 items (top: "src/remediate/contractPipeline/phaseCut.ts:60-100 - derivePhaseCut([{name:'A',depends_on:['B']},{name:'B',depends_on:['A']}]): the outer loop `for (const name o…") — see audit-findings.json for the full list

### COR-8c0a426e — Every failed/skipped 'binary' runner candidate gets two tool_statuses entries, contradicting the documented 'exactly one status per candidate' invariant

resolveBinaryCandidates() pushes an unresolvedStatuses entry for every 'binary' candidate that fails ecosystem-detection, admission, or download resolution.

- Severity: medium
- Confidence: high
- Lens: correctness
- Grounding: grounded
- Files: `src/audit/extractors/analyzers/acquisitionEngine.ts:489–499`, `src/audit/extractors/analyzers/acquisitionEngine.ts:318–332`, `src/audit/orchestrator/acquisitionExecutor.ts:121–127`
- Details: resolveBinaryCandidates() pushes an unresolvedStatuses entry for every 'binary' candidate that fails ecosystem-detection, admission, or download resolution. runAcquisitionEngine() (via runExternalAnalyzer) then unconditionally re-evaluates every registered candidate — including binary ones — and for that SAME failing candidate independently re-derives detect()/admitSpawn() (identical inputs, identical result) and, finding no entry in resolvedBinaries, emits its OWN status object with the same tool id and status ('skipped' or 'not_resolved'). acquisitionExecutor.ts concatenates both arrays (`[...unresolvedStatuses, ...statuses]`) as the marker's tool_statuses, so any binary candidate that fails to resolve is recorded twice, contradicting the code's own comment that the union is 'exactly one status per candidate (report-skipped-never-silently)'. A successfully-resolved binary candidate is unaffected (only one status).
- Evidence: 6 items (top: "src/audit/extractors/analyzers/acquisitionEngine.ts:466-501 - resolveBinaryCandidates pushes exactly one unresolvedStatuses entry per failing binary candidate (…") — see audit-findings.json for the full list

### PRF-03034c94 — Hardcoded MAX_DRAIN_STEPS=64 is a guessed cap whose trip is indistinguishable from a legitimate pause, under a concept name that misleads

Carried from the prior verdict, extended with the short-attention-span perspective's naming observation.

- Severity: medium
- Confidence: high
- Lens: performance
- Grounding: grounded
- Systemic: yes
- Files: `src/audit/orchestrator/advance.ts`, `src/audit/orchestrator/nextStep.ts`, `src/audit/orchestrator/state.ts`
- Details: Carried from the prior verdict, extended with the short-attention-span perspective's naming observation. The obligation-drain loop caps at MAX_DRAIN_STEPS=64 per invocation — a proxy for 'how much work one host round-trip can handle' hardcoded without justification; too low adds round-trips (each paying dist-build check and state-reload overhead), and when the cap DOES trip it returns the same emit shape as a legitimate pause, silently masking a violated no-reopen invariant — thread a distinct 'capped' signal. Prefer a dynamic cap from remaining context budget. Additionally, the concept's public name ('one bounded step per invocation') actively teaches the wrong mental model — the implementation is a fold-aware drain with a soft cap plus a belt-and-braces hard cap that needs multiple comment blocks to justify; renaming to what it is (e.g. 'drain-to-pause') would let the name carry the correct 30-second model instead of three files of corrective prose.
- Evidence: runtime:unit:src-audit: not_confirmed — Deterministic runtime command failed with exit code 1: npm test

### REL-dba7a7c5 — Learned quota limits persist across runs with no drift detection or reset path

Carried from the prior verdict (unaffected by the upstream changes).

- Severity: medium
- Confidence: high
- Lens: reliability
- Grounding: grounded
- Systemic: yes
- Files: `src/shared/quota/learnedQuotaSource.ts`, `src/shared/quota/modelStatics.ts`
- Details: Carried from the prior verdict (unaffected by the upstream changes). Quota learning observes 429/524 errors and learns the actual rate limit dynamically, but a WRONG learned limit (too aggressive or too conservative) has no correction path mid-run: quota state persists in .audit-tools/.quota/ and carries forward across runs, so an incorrect learned limit sticks. A provider's actual quota can change (account upgrade) but the learned value never auto-resets, and manual edits get minimal validation. Solution: add a manual reset mechanism and a drift-detection trigger — if consecutive runs hit the same rate-limit error AFTER applying the learned limit, bump a stale-limit counter and eventually refresh from vendor headers or revert to defaults.
- Evidence: runtime:unit:src-shared: not_confirmed — Deterministic runtime command failed with exit code 1: npm test

Mitigating scope: this is a manually-invoked, operator-attended refresh script (not in any dispatch/CI hot path — package.json wires it only to `update-models`), the failure mode is a visible hang recoverable by Ctrl-C, and the zero-entry guard plus non-ok-status throw already protect against bad data being written. Impact is operator inconvenience, not silent corruption or pipeline failure.

### REL-cc6091dd — Obligation engine assumes all Obligation dependencies are satisfiable, no deadlock detection

The shared obligation engine (src/shared/engine/obligationEngine.ts) drives state transitions by selecting the next actionable Obligation (one with all its depends_on satisfied).

- Severity: medium
- Confidence: high
- Lens: reliability
- Grounding: grounded
- Systemic: yes
- Files: `src/shared/engine/obligationEngine.ts`, `src/shared/types/obligationLedger.ts`, `src/audit/orchestrator/nextStep.ts`, `src/remediate/steps/nextStep.ts`
- Details: The shared obligation engine (src/shared/engine/obligationEngine.ts) drives state transitions by selecting the next actionable Obligation (one with all its depends_on satisfied). The core loop calls findFirstActionableObligation, which iterates obligations and checks if all dependents have already transitioned to a terminal state. However, the contract 'a non-actionable obligation will eventually become actionable' is NEVER validated. If an obligation X depends on obligation Y, and Y's executor (the handler that transitions Y to a terminal state) never runs or always skips Y due to a condition, then X will remain forever non-actionable, and the engine's main loop will cyclically call findFirstActionableObligation, find nothing, and either halt (correctly) or enter an infinite loop (if the loop contract is 're-try forever'). Counterexample: audit-code's obligation cycle has 'provider_confirmation' → 'intention_checkpoint' → ... → 'results_synthesis'. If provider_confirmation is skipped (provider already confirmed, no obligation to fulfill), but intention_checkpoint depends on it, the system would either deadlock or incorrectly treat intention_checkpoint as blocked. The contract 'all dependencies in the ledger must have at least one executor path' is never validated at construction time (detectObligationCycle only checks for cycles, not for unreachable nodes).
- Evidence: 4 items (top: "runtime:unit:src-audit: not_confirmed — Deterministic runtime command failed with exit code 1: npm test") — see audit-findings.json for the full list

- Evidence: 4 items (top: "src/remediate/steps/nextStep.ts:4594-4603 and 4624-4797 - pre-intake obligation IDs are represented once in a priority array and again as object `id` fields; `r…") — see audit-findings.json for the full list

### ARC-6d1638b5 — One-directional shared->orchestrator layering is violated in one verified place, and barrel exports hide the dependency shape

Judge-revised resolution of a contradiction: the prior verdict's critical 'circular import chains' finding and the pragmatist perspective's 17-module-cycle claim both rest on the deterministic cycle findings that DR-001 shows are inflated by a phantom comment-derived edge — so the 'shared, audit, and remediate mutually import each other's barrels' picture is NOT supported at the import level.

- Severity: medium
- Confidence: high
- Lens: architecture
- Grounding: grounded
- Systemic: yes
- Files: `src/shared/contentKey.ts`, `src/shared/index.ts`, `src/audit/orchestrator/artifactFreshness.ts`, `package.json`
- Details: Judge-revised resolution of a contradiction: the prior verdict's critical 'circular import chains' finding and the pragmatist perspective's 17-module-cycle claim both rest on the deterministic cycle findings that DR-001 shows are inflated by a phantom comment-derived edge — so the 'shared, audit, and remediate mutually import each other's barrels' picture is NOT supported at the import level. What survives verification: (1) exactly one real layering violation found by direct grep — src/shared/contentKey.ts imports ../audit/orchestrator/artifactFreshness.js, i.e. shared genuinely reaches into audit; the imported helper (normalizeForMetadataHash) belongs in shared; (2) the structural concern about src/shared/index.ts re-exporting 200+ symbols across ~24 subdirectories stands on its own: importing 'audit-tools/shared' pulls the entire transitive closure and tells the reader nothing about actual dependencies. Recommended: move the artifactFreshness helper into shared and add a mechanical check (lint or test) that src/shared never imports from src/audit or src/remediate — the dependency arrow one-directional by construction, not convention; then split shared into narrower subpath exports (shared/io, shared/quota, shared/providers) so consumers declare what they actually use. Re-run the cycle report after DR-001's edge-kind fix to see whether any true import cycle remains.
- Evidence: 3 items (top: "runtime:unit:src-audit: not_confirmed — Deterministic runtime command failed with exit code 1: npm test") — see audit-findings.json for the full list

### ARC-5b84d9b4 — Path disposition logic is a cascade of 16+ conditionals; should be table-driven

Carried from the prior verdict (unaffected by the upstream changes).

- Severity: medium
- Confidence: high
- Lens: architecture
- Grounding: grounded
- Files: `src/audit/extractors/disposition.ts`, `src/audit/extractors/pathPatterns.ts`
- Details: Carried from the prior verdict (unaffected by the upstream changes). src/audit/extractors/disposition.ts encodes file-path classification as nested if statements (isNodeModulesOrGit, isPackageManagerCachePath, isTmpPath, isBuildOutput, isVendorPath, ...), each calling a predicate from pathPatterns.ts. New patterns require new functions plus new conditionals, and priority order is invisible. The right abstraction is a table [{pattern, status, reason}] iterated in priority order returning first match — separating classification (one loop) from the pattern library (data), shrinking the file substantially and making the priority order explicit and testable.
- Evidence: runtime:unit:src-audit: not_confirmed — Deterministic runtime command failed with exit code 1: npm test

### ARC-b9c0b3ea — Provider-agnostic architecture violates by importing provider-specific enums in orchestrators

The shared module exports PROVIDER_NAMES (src/shared/types/sessionConfig.ts line 277) as a hard-coded set of provider name strings.

- Severity: medium
- Confidence: high
- Lens: architecture
- Grounding: grounded
- Systemic: yes
- Files: `src/shared/types/sessionConfig.ts`, `src/audit/providers/index.ts`, `src/remediate/providers/index.ts`, `src/shared/providers/providerFactory.ts`
- Details: The shared module exports PROVIDER_NAMES (src/shared/types/sessionConfig.ts line 277) as a hard-coded set of provider name strings. Both audit and remediate orchestrators import this enum and construct provider instances keyed by name (src/audit/providers/index.ts, src/remediate/providers/index.ts). This appears provider-agnostic until a new provider is added: the string must be added to PROVIDER_NAMES, the factory must be updated, and both orchestrators' provider/index.ts must conditionally construct it. The claim 'language-neutral' and 'provider-agnostic by default' (CLAUDE.md) is violated: adding a provider requires changes to 4 files (shared enum + two orchestrator factories). A truly provider-agnostic system would discover providers via configuration or plugin registration, not hard-coded enums. Counterexample: if a third-party wanted to add a custom provider backend at runtime (via environment variable or session config), the current architecture would refuse it — the provider name must be in the pre-compiled PROVIDER_NAMES set.
- Evidence: 8 items (top: "runtime:unit:src-audit: not_confirmed — Deterministic runtime command failed with exit code 1: npm test") — see audit-findings.json for the full list

No defect in this task's target files; the runtime failure is test-environment contamination, not source behavior. The assertion cannot distinguish 'cmdQuota wrote the file now' from 'the file already existed'; it should snapshot pre-existence (or assert on mtime/absence delta), otherwise every runtime/self-audit `npm test` on a working copy with an active .audit-tools session reports a false regression — which is exactly what fanned out 14 runtime-reconcile deepening tasks in this run.

### COR-6c396ba9 — referencedFiles contract assumes files on disk but doesn't validate before dispatch

LaunchFreshSessionInput.referencedFiles (src/shared/providers/types.ts:40-51) documents 'the authoritative set of files the worker is granted to READ' with 'a member that does not exist on disk is a to-be-created file and is NOT a failure.' This means a referenced_files entry can point to a nonexistent path, and the worker is expected to write it from scratch.

- Severity: medium
- Confidence: high
- Lens: correctness
- Grounding: grounded
- Files: `src/shared/providers/types.ts`, `src/shared/providers/openAiCompatibleProvider.ts`, `src/remediate/steps/dispatch/marshal.ts`, `src/audit/cli/dispatch/packetPrompt.ts`
- Details: LaunchFreshSessionInput.referencedFiles (src/shared/providers/types.ts:40-51) documents 'the authoritative set of files the worker is granted to READ' with 'a member that does not exist on disk is a to-be-created file and is NOT a failure.' This means a referenced_files entry can point to a nonexistent path, and the worker is expected to write it from scratch. However, the openai-compatible provider (src/shared/providers/openAiCompatibleProvider.ts) is documented to inline file CONTENTS for single-shot workers. The contract has a gap: what happens if a referenced file does not exist when the openai-compatible provider tries to inline it? A counterexample dispatch: remediate calls openai-compatible provider with referencedFiles=['new-file.ts'] (a file to be created). The provider's content-inlining code reads the file, gets ENOENT, and must either (1) skip it silently (losing the grant context), (2) inline an empty stub, or (3) fail. The current code is not clear which; the contract does not specify the behavior for nonexistent read-grants. This creates a silent contract violation: the worker is told it can READ new-file.ts (per the grant), but the content is unavailable because the file doesn't exist yet. A worker implementing `readFile('new-file.ts')` would work against an agentic CLI provider (claude-code, which has access to the real file) but fail against openai-compatible (which inlined nothing).
- Evidence: 3 items (top: "runtime:unit:src-audit: not_confirmed — Deterministic runtime command failed with exit code 1: npm test") — see audit-findings.json for the full list

### OPR-a05e19e4 — Release pipeline is a single 655-line, CC=158, non-resumable script chaining external network calls

Merged from the prior verdict (procedural complexity) and the adversary perspective (non-resumability), which sharpens the impact: main() runs a strictly sequential, non-idempotent chain — pre-tag gate -> version bump + local tag -> push commit -> push tag + gh release create -> poll CI -> poll npm — with no checkpoint file.

- Severity: medium
- Confidence: high
- Lens: operability
- Grounding: grounded
- Files: `scripts/release-and-publish.mjs`
- Details: Merged from the prior verdict (procedural complexity) and the adversary perspective (non-resumability), which sharpens the impact: main() runs a strictly sequential, non-idempotent chain — pre-tag gate -> version bump + local tag -> push commit -> push tag + gh release create -> poll CI -> poll npm — with no checkpoint file. Any failure between the local tag and completion (network blip, gh auth expiry, GitHub 5xx) leaves a state a bare re-run cannot recover: npm version double-bumps and git tag -a fails on the surviving local tag; recovery is manual git/tag archaeology (the project's own memory already documents a recovery procedure, confirming recurrence). Fix both axes together: decompose into per-step functions with error handling baked in (bumpVersion, commitTag, pushTags, createRelease, waitForCiRun, waitForNpm) sequenced by a thin orchestrator, and persist an on-disk phase marker so a re-invocation detects and resumes/repairs a partial prior run instead of re-deriving intent from git state by hand.
- Evidence: runtime:flow:flow:host:npm-release-publish: not_confirmed — Deterministic runtime command failed with exit code 1: npm test

### COR-96f1b917 — releaseLock read-then-unlink is non-atomic: can delete another holder's freshly stolen lock

releaseLock reads the lock content, compares it to the owner token, then unlinks in a separate step.

- Severity: medium
- Confidence: high
- Lens: correctness
- Grounding: grounded
- Files: `src/shared/quota/fileLock.ts:200–212`
- Details: releaseLock reads the lock content, compares it to the owner token, then unlinks in a separate step. If the holder's own lock has gone stale (>30s hold) and a concurrent acquirer steals it and creates a FRESH lock between the read (which matched our token) and the unlink, releaseLock deletes the new holder's lock — the exact two-step pattern stealStaleLock's comment documents as admitting a double-hold, fixed for the steal path but retained verbatim here.
- Evidence: 5 items (top: "src/shared/quota/fileLock.ts:202-207 - read, compare, then unlink as three separate awaited steps with no exclusivity between them.") — see audit-findings.json for the full list

### COR-4b8517d1 — retainAutoExclusions include-matching uses the retired bare pattern grammar, so an operator opt-in never lifts an axis-explicit auto-exclusion

retainAutoExclusions decides whether an operator `include` addresses a gate-authored auto-exclusion by testing `pattern === provider || pattern.startsWith(provider + ':')`, which is the pre-stage-4 bare grammar (`codex`, `codex:model`).

- Severity: medium
- Confidence: high
- Lens: correctness
- Grounding: grounded
- Files: `src/shared/providers/sharedProviderConfirmation.ts:1633–1643`, `src/shared/providers/identity.ts:122–129`
- Details: retainAutoExclusions decides whether an operator `include` addresses a gate-authored auto-exclusion by testing `pattern === provider || pattern.startsWith(provider + ':')`, which is the pre-stage-4 bare grammar (`codex`, `codex:model`). Gate-authored auto-exclusions are always axis-explicit (`transport:codex/...` via exclusionPattern, `service:.../...` via serviceExclusionPattern), so neither test ever matches: `'transport:codex' === 'codex'` is false and `'transport:codex'.startsWith('codex:')` is false. The documented contract ('the operator named that provider in include — an explicit opt-back-IN' supersedes the auto-exclusion) is therefore never honored: the auto-exclusion is retained on every promotion, and since resolveDispatchExclusion merges auto_exclude unconditionally (include only lifts the self-spawn fold), the opted-in backend stays excluded at dispatch indefinitely. Fail-closed direction, but it silently discards an explicit operator decision — the exact silence class the module's own docs prohibit.

### PRF-c9869af2 — Run external analyzer candidates concurrently instead of one spawnSync at a time

runAcquisitionEngine (src/audit/extractors/analyzers/acquisitionEngine.ts) drives every registered ExternalAnalyzerCandidate (gitleaks, semgrep, eslint, knip, jscpd, osv-scanner, clippy, rubocop, hadolint, actionlint, type-coverage — 11 candidates in src/audit/extractors/analyzers/candidates.ts) through a plain `for` loop calling runExternalAnalyzer synchronously for each.

- Severity: medium
- Confidence: high
- Lens: performance
- Grounding: grounded
- Systemic: yes
- Files: `src/audit/extractors/analyzers/acquisitionEngine.ts`, `src/audit/extractors/analyzers/candidates.ts`, `src/shared/tooling/exec.ts`
- Details: runAcquisitionEngine (src/audit/extractors/analyzers/acquisitionEngine.ts) drives every registered ExternalAnalyzerCandidate (gitleaks, semgrep, eslint, knip, jscpd, osv-scanner, clippy, rubocop, hadolint, actionlint, type-coverage — 11 candidates in src/audit/extractors/analyzers/candidates.ts) through a plain `for` loop calling runExternalAnalyzer synchronously for each. runExternalAnalyzer's runSafetyGate does a blocking capability probe (`run(runnerProbeArgv(...))`) and then the tool spawn itself both go through runTracked (src/shared/tooling/exec.ts), which is built on Node's synchronous `spawnSync` — so each candidate fully blocks the process (npx/pipx/cargo acquisition + tool execution) before the next one even starts its probe. Each candidate is independent: separate runner (npx/pipx/cargo/bundle/binary), separate argv, separate output (own report file or stdout), no shared mutable state, and the own-vs-acquire/consent-gating logic (admitSpawn, run-safety gate) is evaluated per-candidate with no cross-candidate dependency. This is the same 'isolated child processes waited on one at a time' shape already flagged for the two postinstall sub-deployers (postinstall-sequential-spawn-serialization), but in a functionally different subsystem — the on-demand external-analyzer acquisition engine that runs during audit extraction, not install — and with a larger fan-out (up to 11 tools vs. 2 scripts), so a polyglot repo triggering several ecosystems (Node+Python+Rust+Ruby+Docker+GitHub Actions) pays the full sum of every tool's acquire+run latency serially. Switching runExternalAnalyzer's tool-spawn step to the async `spawn`/`execFile` equivalent (keeping the synchronous probe or making it async too) and running the per-candidate work through `Promise.all`/a small concurrency pool would collapse total acquisition wall time from sum(tool_times) to max(tool_times), directly speeding up every audit run that touches more than one ecosystem.
- Evidence: 2 items (top: "runtime:unit:src-audit: not_confirmed — Deterministic runtime command failed with exit code 1: npm test") — see audit-findings.json for the full list

### TST-4e53f6e9 — seam-host-only-next-step exhaustiveness check compares two hand-duplicated literal sets, not the real source

The file's docstring claims 'This test fails whenever either side diverges from the shared interface', but RETURN_KINDS_FROM_NEXT_STEP_HELPERS and CMD_NEXT_STEP_HANDLED_KINDS are both hand-maintained literal Set()s declared inline in the test file (per its own comment: 'without importing the function body').

- Severity: medium
- Confidence: high
- Lens: tests
- Grounding: grounded
- Files: `tests/audit/seam-host-only-next-step.test.mjs:17`, `tests/audit/seam-host-only-next-step.test.mjs:90–95`, `tests/audit/seam-host-only-next-step.test.mjs:110–112`
- Details: The file's docstring claims 'This test fails whenever either side diverges from the shared interface', but RETURN_KINDS_FROM_NEXT_STEP_HELPERS and CMD_NEXT_STEP_HANDLED_KINDS are both hand-maintained literal Set()s declared inline in the test file (per its own comment: 'without importing the function body'). Tests A1/A2/A3 only check the two literal arrays against each other, never against the real return-type union in nextStepHelpers.ts or the real if/else branches in cmdNextStep. If the two production files actually diverge (a new kind added to one but not the other), this test cannot detect it -- it only detects if the test author forgot to keep the two copies in this file synced with each other. The real fix is the duplication itself: derive both sets from the production source (e.g. import the literal union type via a runtime-reachable const array, or grep/parse nextStepCommand.ts's branches) so the guard is unnecessary and the test exercises the real seam it claims to protect.
- Evidence: 6 items (top: "tests/audit/seam-host-only-next-step.test.mjs:17 - docstring asserts the test 'fails whenever either side diverges from the shared interface'") — see audit-findings.json for the full list

### COR-e3dd9a7f — Space-delimited pair keys corrupt valid paths containing spaces

- Grounding: grounded
- Files: `src/audit/extractors/commentDecomposition.ts:284–294`, `src/audit/extractors/dataStateCoupling.ts:84–97`
- Details: Both coupling extractors serialize a pair as `a + space + b` and later split at the first space. Because repository paths may legally contain spaces, a pair such as `src/my file.ts` and `src/z.ts` is reconstructed as the nonexistent endpoints `src/my` and `file.ts src/z.ts`.
- Evidence: 3 items (top: "src/audit/extractors/commentDecomposition.ts:284-294 - canonical paths are joined with a literal space and decoded using the first literal space.") — see audit-findings.json for the full list

### REL-38c41e7e — Staleness dependency DAG not enforced at refresh time

The audit system (CLAUDE.md) documents 'Artifacts are continuity; dependency DAG is truth' and defines explicit staleness ordering in spec/audit/dependency-map.md and src/audit/orchestrator/staleness.ts.

- Severity: medium
- Confidence: high
- Lens: reliability
- Grounding: grounded
- Systemic: yes
- Files: `src/audit/orchestrator/staleness.ts`, `src/audit/orchestrator/nextStep.ts`, `src/audit/orchestrator/artifactMetadata.ts`, `spec/audit/dependency-map.md`
- Details: The audit system (CLAUDE.md) documents 'Artifacts are continuity; dependency DAG is truth' and defines explicit staleness ordering in spec/audit/dependency-map.md and src/audit/orchestrator/staleness.ts. However, no runtime validation exists to ensure that when a downstream artifact is refreshed, all upstream dependencies are already valid (not stale). The rule 'upstream-valid before downstream-refresh' is a precondition for correct audits, but it is enforced only by convention in the orchestrator's obligation ordering. Counterexample: repo_manifest depends on file_inventory. If a developer manually deletes file_inventory.json from .audit-tools/audit/, then calls next-step, the obligation engine should refuse to refresh repo_manifest (it is stale). However, if the obligation order happens to queue 'repo_manifest_extraction' before 'file_inventory_extraction' due to a typo or reordering, the orchestrator will compute an invalid repo_manifest against stale inventory data. The PRIORITY array in nextStep.ts single-sources the ordering, but there is no compile-time or runtime assertion that the order respects the dependency DAG. A corrupted session could manifest dependency violations that silently propagate through subsequent analyses.
- Evidence: 2 items (top: "runtime:unit:src-audit: not_confirmed — Deterministic runtime command failed with exit code 1: npm test") — see audit-findings.json for the full list

### COR-756614b5 — Stray worktree detection is reactive, not preventive; accepts work from the wrong tree

Carried from the prior verdict (unaffected by the upstream changes).

- Severity: medium
- Confidence: high
- Lens: correctness
- Grounding: grounded
- Files: `src/remediate/steps/dispatch/acceptNode.ts`, `src/remediate/steps/dispatch/worktreeLifecycle.ts`
- Details: Carried from the prior verdict (unaffected by the upstream changes). The remediate dispatch system detects stray worktrees AFTER editing: when a node claims an edit but the designated worktree has zero commits, strayWorktreeSuspected is flagged in AcceptNodeWorktreeResult — by which point the risk has materialized (the agent edited in a tree the dispatch system didn't control). Prevention is the correct shape: verify before/at dispatch that the worker's effective worktree IS the designated one (compare identity markers), so a second spawned worktree fails fast instead of producing unmergeable work.
- Evidence: runtime:unit:src-remediate: not_confirmed — Deterministic runtime command failed with exit code 1: npm test

The result: applyTemplate's 'unknown placeholder' error (a real misconfiguration signal — a typo'd template token silently renders as an empty argv element) is logged nowhere an operator can see it in normal use.
- Evidence: 5 items (top: "src/shared/providers/subprocessTemplateProvider.ts:98 - this.log = runLogger ?? RunLogger.disabled(): with no injected logger every event() call becomes a no-op…") — see audit-findings.json for the full list

### MNT-4058da4e — The contract-pipeline artifact payload shapes (goal_spec/finalized_module_contracts/etc) are hand-duplicated across 5 test files and have already drifted

Each of these 5 files defines its own local builder function for the same contract-pipeline artifact payloads (goal_spec, finalized_module_contracts, module_contracts, etc.) instead of importing one shared fixture module.

- Severity: medium
- Confidence: high
- Lens: maintainability
- Grounding: grounded
- Systemic: yes
- Files: `tests/remediate/contract-pipeline-artifact-store.test.ts`, `tests/remediate/contract-pipeline-cp-node-2.test.ts`, `tests/remediate/contract-pipeline-derive-obligations.test.ts`, `tests/remediate/contract-pipeline-diff-review.test.ts` +1 more
- Details: Each of these 5 files defines its own local builder function for the same contract-pipeline artifact payloads (goal_spec, finalized_module_contracts, module_contracts, etc.) instead of importing one shared fixture module. The duplication is not just repetitive but has already drifted: contract-pipeline-diff-review.test.ts's finalized_module_contracts fixture omits the `side_effects` field that every other file's copy includes, and adds a `rationale` field none of the others carry. One .mjs implementation importable by wrapper, build, and (re-exported) shared code would remove both the parity test and the weak copy.

Worse, it uses RECURSIVE path compression, while UnionFind.find was deliberately rewritten to be ITERATIVE specifically to avoid unbounded recursion on degenerate union chains (per its own in-file comment, tagged COR-b6f68ad7) — a fix this independent copy does not inherit. The same union-find logic now exists in two places kept consistent only by convention, and one of the two copies has silently regressed a previously-fixed defect class.

### COR-fc991e7c — `npm run generate-schemas` omits the tsx loader the script requires, so it fails on Node 20/22 with ERR_UNKNOWN_FILE_EXTENSION

- Severity: medium
- Confidence: medium
- Lens: correctness
- Grounding: grounded
- Files: `scripts/audit/generate-schemas.mjs:1–16`, `package.json:59`
- Details: generate-schemas.mjs imports a .ts module directly and its own header documents the required invocation `node --import tsx/esm ...`, but the package script wires it as plain `node scripts/audit/generate-schemas.mjs`. On Node 20/22 (both allowed by engines >=20) plain node cannot import .ts files, so the documented npm entrypoint fails; it only works on Node versions with default type stripping.
- Evidence: 5 items (top: "scripts/audit/generate-schemas.mjs:3 - the header documents the required invocation: `node --import tsx/esm scripts/audit/generate-schemas.mjs`.") — see audit-findings.json for the full list

### ARC-b9b64cd4 — Adapter pattern for external tools lacks a shared extraction template

Carried from the prior verdict (unaffected by the upstream changes).

- Severity: medium
- Confidence: medium
- Lens: architecture
- Grounding: grounded
- Files: `src/audit/adapters/eslint.ts`, `src/audit/adapters/clippy.ts`, `src/audit/adapters/semgrep.ts`
- Details: Carried from the prior verdict (unaffected by the upstream changes). Each external analyzer adapter (eslint.ts, clippy.ts, semgrep.ts, ...) re-implements the same template: extract results -> map severity -> normalize -> call normalizeGenericExternalResults. A parameterizable adapter factory — AdapterFactory(input, fieldMap {filePath, severity, line, message}, severityMapper) — could handle most cases and reduce each adapter to configuration, so new acquired tools are added by providing config rather than writing adapter code. Lower priority than the structural findings, but a real composability gap in the own-vs-acquire pipeline.
- Evidence: runtime:unit:src-audit: not_confirmed — Deterministic runtime command failed with exit code 1: npm test

### REL-965de341 — Admitted broker repair slot is never completed/released when the patcher throws

brokeredPatcher offers a slot to broker.broker() and, once admitted, awaits input.patcher before calling broker.awaitNextCompletion.

- Severity: medium
- Confidence: medium
- Lens: reliability
- Grounding: grounded
- Files: `src/audit/contracts/schemaEnforcedEmit.ts:169–187`
- Details: brokeredPatcher offers a slot to broker.broker() and, once admitted, awaits input.patcher before calling broker.awaitNextCompletion. There is no try/finally: if the patcher (an LLM call) rejects or times out, the exception propagates out of the brokered patcher with the admitted slot's reservation never completed, so the broker's budget/lease accounting for that slot is stranded — subsequent repairs against the same pool see less admissible budget than actually exists.
- Evidence: 3 items (top: "src/audit/contracts/schemaEnforcedEmit.ts:171-176 - broker.broker() admits the slot (reserving against the repair budget) before the patcher runs.") — see audit-findings.json for the full list

### TST-3a5d235f — auditRegisteredHostFlags() proves only that a string occurs in the file, not that a flag is registered

Unlike remediateRegisteredHostFlags(), which introspects the live commander program's registered options, auditRegisteredHostFlags() regex-scans raw source text for the flag string.

- Severity: medium
- Confidence: medium
- Lens: tests
- Grounding: grounded
- Files: `tests/remediate/cli-host-capability-flags.test.ts:90–99`
- Details: Unlike remediateRegisteredHostFlags(), which introspects the live commander program's registered options, auditRegisteredHostFlags() regex-scans raw source text for the flag string. A match inside a comment or an unused string constant would satisfy the assertion the same as a real .option() registration, and a genuine registration built via a template literal or shared array (a syntax the regex doesn't anticipate) would go undetected - the two halves of the same parity check use materially different-strength techniques.
- Evidence: 4 items (top: "tests/remediate/cli-host-capability-flags.test.ts:81-88 - remediateRegisteredHostFlags() reads flags off the real nextStepCommand().options, introspecting actua…") — see audit-findings.json for the full list

### TST-c55d7de2 — auto-fix-executor.test.mjs's failed-formatter tests assert only the tool name, never any diagnostic detail — because the code under test captures none

auto-fix-executor.test.mjs has multiple dedicated tests for the formatter-failure path (a stub prettier binary exiting non-zero) and asserts the tool name lands in failed_tools and that progress_summary mentions 'Formatters failed:' — but no test anywhere asserts on an error message, exit code, or stderr snippet for the failure, because tryRunConfiguredFormatter() in the source under test discards that information entirely.

- Severity: medium
- Confidence: medium
- Lens: tests
- Grounding: grounded
- Files: `src/audit/orchestrator/autoFixExecutor.ts:12–20`
- Details: auto-fix-executor.test.mjs has multiple dedicated tests for the formatter-failure path (a stub prettier binary exiting non-zero) and asserts the tool name lands in failed_tools and that progress_summary mentions 'Formatters failed:' — but no test anywhere asserts on an error message, exit code, or stderr snippet for the failure, because tryRunConfiguredFormatter() in the source under test discards that information entirely. The suite thoroughly pins the current (diagnostic-free) shape of a formatter failure without ever asserting that diagnostic detail exists, so it would not catch a future change that silently drops even more context, and it does not document the missing-diagnostics gap as an intentional limitation.
- Evidence: 4 items (top: "src/audit/orchestrator/autoFixExecutor.ts:16-19 - tryRunConfiguredFormatter collapses the full command result to the bare string "failed", so there is no diagno…") — see audit-findings.json for the full list

### DAT-eff086cb — buildWorkerRepairContract's validation semantics contradict its documented contract: every zod issue is marked required, including optional-field errors

The function's JSDoc states that errors on a field that is optional() in the schema map to required: false so O3's stage-1 coercion may clear them by dropping the optional sub-object.

- Severity: medium
- Confidence: medium
- Lens: data_integrity
- Grounding: grounded
- Files: `src/audit/contracts/schemaEnforcedEmit.ts:40–50`, `src/audit/contracts/schemaEnforcedEmit.ts:60–72`
- Details: The function's JSDoc states that errors on a field that is optional() in the schema map to required: false so O3's stage-1 coercion may clear them by dropping the optional sub-object. The implementation unconditionally sets required: true for every zod issue (a present-but-malformed optional field DOES produce zod issues). Consequence: a payload whose only defect is a malformed optional field is escalation-gated to the bounded LLM patch / re-dispatch path instead of being deterministically repaired by dropping the optional sub-object — the opposite of the documented degrade order at this validation boundary.
- Evidence: 3 items (top: "src/audit/contracts/schemaEnforcedEmit.ts:41-43 - the documented contract: optional-field errors map to required: false.") — see audit-findings.json for the full list

- Severity: medium

- Grounding: grounded
- Systemic: yes
- Files: `scripts/audit/smoke-linked-audit-code.mjs`, `scripts/audit/smoke-packaged-audit-code.mjs`
- Details: The stated purpose is restricted to verifying, before publish, that the *linked* (development) audit code loads without fatal import errors, singling out src-level rename/regression bugs. The revealed purpose requires symmetric coverage of both `npm linked` AND tarball-installed modes and full lifecycle driving (report presentation, restart). The stated purpose's exclusive focus on the linked-source import path and 'before publish' timing directly contradicts the revealed purpose's requirement that the tool run equally well as a tarball-installed package without human intervention.
- Evidence: 2 items (top: "runtime:unit:scripts-audit: not_confirmed — Deterministic runtime command failed with exit code 1: npm test") — see audit-findings.json for the full list

### COR-8c3956e7 — ClaudeWorkerProvider silently downgrades to the keyless dummy token when api_key_env is configured but unset

When config.api_key_env is set but the referenced environment variable is absent/empty, ClaudeWorkerProvider falls back to CLAUDE_WORKER_DUMMY_API_KEY with no error, even though the class doc explicitly reserves that sentinel for the 'keyless' (no api_key_env configured) case.

- Severity: medium
- Confidence: medium
- Lens: correctness
- Grounding: grounded
- Files: `src/shared/providers/claudeWorkerProvider.ts:171–175`, `src/shared/providers/claudeWorkerProvider.ts:24–31`
- Details: When config.api_key_env is set but the referenced environment variable is absent/empty, ClaudeWorkerProvider falls back to CLAUDE_WORKER_DUMMY_API_KEY with no error, even though the class doc explicitly reserves that sentinel for the 'keyless' (no api_key_env configured) case. The sibling OpenAiCompatibleProvider fails loudly in the exact analogous situation, so an operator who configured a credential that happens to be unset in this process silently gets an unauthenticated request instead of a clear configuration error.
- Evidence: 3 items (top: "src/shared/providers/claudeWorkerProvider.ts:171-175 - authKeyValue is undefined whenever api_key_env is configured but process.env[api_key_env] is unset/empty,…") — see audit-findings.json for the full list

### ARC-6704297b-2 — Complexity/duplication analyzer is a JS/TS-only regex heuristic despite an in-repo multi-language parser and available acquired tools

Raised by the novelty perspective.

- Severity: medium
- Confidence: medium
- Lens: architecture
- Grounding: grounded
- Files: `src/audit/extractors/analyzers/complexityDuplication.ts`, `src/audit/extractors/analyzers/treeSitter.ts`, `src/audit/adapters/eslint.ts`
- Details: Raised by the novelty perspective. src/audit/extractors/analyzers/complexityDuplication.ts computes 'cyclomatic-approx' by regex-counting branch keywords and detects duplication by hashing normalized lines, gated to JS/TS paths only — silently returning no signal for every other language. This sits against two facts already true in the repo: web-tree-sitter + tree-sitter-wasms are already dependencies used for real multi-language grammars (treeSitter.ts), and duplication detection has a purpose-built multi-language acquired tool (jscpd) that fits the project's documented own-vs-acquire policy of normalizing ecosystem-native analyzers into leads. As is, the `high_complexity` and `duplicated_code` risk signals feeding risk scoring are silently absent for non-JS/TS repos — the opposite of the language-neutral-by-contract principle, and a silent-degradation shape the project elsewhere treats as a bug. Either extend via the existing tree-sitter infrastructure or acquire jscpd through the adapter path.
- Evidence: runtime:unit:src-audit: not_confirmed — Deterministic runtime command failed with exit code 1: npm test

### ARC-d3d6f01b — Concurrency correctness is accreting as per-incident patches, not one verified model

Raised by the adversary perspective.

- Severity: medium
- Confidence: medium
- Lens: architecture
- Grounding: grounded
- Systemic: yes
- Files: `src/shared/quota/fileLock.ts`, `src/remediate/steps/dispatch/worktreeLifecycle.ts`, `src/shared/quota/reservationLedger.ts`
- Details: Raised by the adversary perspective. The most concurrency-critical files (fileLock.ts, worktreeLifecycle.ts, reservationLedger.ts, claimLease.ts) are dense with individually-reasoned invariant comments (INV-WTS-1..8, CE-001/003/004, OD1/OD3, the steal-claim rewrite documenting the prior double-hold race). Each is correct for the race it was written against, but the pattern is reactive: an interleaving is observed in production, a narrow fix is documented in place, and the next unanticipated interleaving surfaces elsewhere in the same family. DR-003 (lock staleness vs critical-section duration) is itself a plausible race the existing invariants do not cover. As the project commits to more concurrent writers joining one run, the surface for new interleavings grows faster than one-bug-at-a-time patching. Recommended: consolidate the cross-process state-mutation paths (state.json, session-config.json, quota ledgers) onto one audited primitive — e.g. compare-and-swap writes keyed on a monotonic version — verified once and reused, rather than special-casing each new file needing cross-process safety.
- Evidence: 4 items (top: "runtime:unit:src-remediate: not_confirmed — Deterministic runtime command failed with exit code 1: npm test") — see audit-findings.json for the full list

### OBS-6ba55c63 — Dispatch admission-wall, host-pause and livelock transitions carry no run-log telemetry

dispatch.ts's host-pause block, hostFanoutGate.ts's atWall handling, and pausePersist.ts's livelock branch all mutate JSON state artifacts on a quota wall / livelock give-up but never call RunLogger, unlike the project's own precedent in dispatchStatusCommand.ts (comment 'FND-OBS-6e84f23c') which logs its read-only status checks to run.log.jsonl — so an operator reconstructing a run's timeline from run.log.jsonl cannot see when or why a dispatch walled, paused, or gave up to livelock.

### REL-b3f6ac48 — Every parallel-dispatch worktree shares one real node_modules via junction

Raised by the adversary perspective.

- Severity: medium
- Confidence: medium
- Lens: reliability
- Grounding: grounded
- Systemic: yes
- Files: `src/remediate/steps/dispatch/worktreeLifecycle.ts`
- Details: Raised by the adversary perspective. `ensureWorktreeNodeModules` (src/remediate/steps/dispatch/worktreeLifecycle.ts) links each per-node worktree's node_modules to the MAIN checkout's via junction/symlink. Parallel dispatch over overlapping files is the stated goal, but every concurrent node's verify/test/build invocation executes against the same physical node_modules: shared .bin shims, shared tool caches (node_modules/.cache, .vite), shared resolution state. One worker running npm install/ci — or any build tool writing into node_modules as a side effect — mutates state every sibling's concurrent verify depends on mid-flight, a shared-mutable-resource hazard that scales with exactly the concurrency dimension the architecture is trying to increase. The in-code comment acknowledges cross-package resolution but not concurrent-write corruption. Options: read-only link/overlay, explicitly forbidding node_modules mutation inside worker targeted_commands with violation detection, or per-worktree installs for isolation-sensitive nodes.
- Evidence: runtime:unit:src-remediate: not_confirmed — Deterministic runtime command failed with exit code 1: npm test

### MNT-2a9f167b — First-party User-Agent strings duplicated in openCodeQuotaSource, kept consistent by comment convention

ROUTE_USER_AGENT hardcodes the per-provider first-party UA strings with the comment 'match the standalone sources' defaults' — the same literals each standalone source (claudeOAuth/codex/copilot) declares as its own default.

- Severity: medium
- Confidence: medium
- Lens: maintainability
- Grounding: grounded
- Files: `src/shared/quota/openCodeQuotaSource.ts:32–37`
- Details: ROUTE_USER_AGENT hardcodes the per-provider first-party UA strings with the comment 'match the standalone sources' defaults' — the same literals each standalone source (claudeOAuth/codex/copilot) declares as its own default. The invariant 'these must match' is enforced only by convention: changing a standalone source's UA (e.g. because the anthropic endpoint tightens UA-based rate-limiting, which the comment itself says it does aggressively) silently leaves the opencode-broker route on the stale string. Single-source each default as an export of its source module and import it here.
- Evidence: 2 items (top: "src/shared/quota/openCodeQuotaSource.ts:32-37 - three UA literals with a comment promising they match the standalone sources' defaults; nothing mechanical ties …") — see audit-findings.json for the full list

### COR-a6e78788 — Global OpenCode bash table in remediate postinstall is stale: allows retired document-phase commands and omits accept-node

OPENCODE_REMEDIATE_BASH_PERMISSION in scripts/remediate/postinstall.mjs still allows the document-phase commands (prepare-document-dispatch, merge-document-results) — a phase the project record says was dissolved (N-R13) — and does NOT allow 'remediate-code accept-node*', which is a live workflow command: the project-scope table in wrapper/remediate-code-wrapper-opencode.mjs seeds it and assertOpenCodeRemediatePermissionConfig requires it.

- Severity: medium
- Confidence: medium
- Lens: correctness
- Grounding: grounded
- Files: `scripts/remediate/postinstall.mjs:44–67`, `wrapper/remediate-code-wrapper-opencode.mjs:29–36`
- Details: OPENCODE_REMEDIATE_BASH_PERMISSION in scripts/remediate/postinstall.mjs still allows the document-phase commands (prepare-document-dispatch, merge-document-results) — a phase the project record says was dissolved (N-R13) — and does NOT allow 'remediate-code accept-node*', which is a live workflow command: the project-scope table in wrapper/remediate-code-wrapper-opencode.mjs seeds it and assertOpenCodeRemediatePermissionConfig requires it. So the globally-seeded config prompts ('ask') on accept-node while granting rules for commands that no longer exist. The two managed tables for the same tool have drifted.
- Evidence: 3 items (top: "scripts/remediate/postinstall.mjs:44-67 - the global managed table lists prepare-document-dispatch*/merge-document-results*/validate-artifacts* and contains no …") — see audit-findings.json for the full list

### DAT-017d52ff-2 — Host capability handshake (C1) persists unvalidated JSON without schema

RemediationState.host_capabilities (src/remediate/state/store.ts:55, defined as `HostCapabilities | undefined`) is intentionally 'opaque to the state store: stored and round-tripped verbatim, never interpreted here.' However, validateState (line 127-142) only validates that 'status' field is known; it does NOT validate the shape of host_capabilities.

- Severity: medium
- Confidence: medium
- Lens: data_integrity
- Grounding: grounded
- Files: `src/remediate/state/store.ts`, `src/remediate/steps/sessionConfigLoad.ts`, `src/remediate/steps/nextStep.ts`
- Details: RemediationState.host_capabilities (src/remediate/state/store.ts:55, defined as `HostCapabilities | undefined`) is intentionally 'opaque to the state store: stored and round-tripped verbatim, never interpreted here.' However, validateState (line 127-142) only validates that 'status' field is known; it does NOT validate the shape of host_capabilities. A malformed or version-drifted host_capabilities object persists unchanged and could cause downstream errors if a later step attempts to read specific fields (e.g., max_concurrent expecting a number but receiving a string). The C1 protocol says 'explicitly-supplied fields only' and 'omitted field never clobbers,' but there is no Zod schema enforcing the shape of supplied fields (no rejection of unexpected keys, no type coercion). Counterexample: A future version of the host protocol may introduce a new field max_dispatch_latency_ms; an old remediator reading state persisted by a new host would not see it (fields are opaque), but a new remediator reading state persisted by an old host would lack it. If the code ever assumes `state.host_capabilities!.max_concurrent`, it would execute without error on stale state and silently default to undefined behavior (the contract is 'present-but-unvalidated'). No per-field validation schema exists to guard this contract.
- Evidence: 5 items (top: "runtime:unit:src-remediate: not_confirmed — Deterministic runtime command failed with exit code 1: npm test") — see audit-findings.json for the full list

### OBS-7dcadeb1 — Host-delegation dispatch obligation has no liveness/progress marker

executeAndRecord writes a `steps/deterministic-progress.json` started/running/complete heartbeat around every deterministic executor dispatch (explicitly to let a host watching the filesystem tell which executor is active during a long-running step, per OBS-0d4c2311).

- Severity: medium
- Confidence: medium
- Lens: observability
- Grounding: grounded
- Files: `src/audit/cli/nextStepHelpers.ts:1415–1424`, `src/audit/cli/nextStepHelpers.ts:2098`
- Details: executeAndRecord writes a `steps/deterministic-progress.json` started/running/complete heartbeat around every deterministic executor dispatch (explicitly to let a host watching the filesystem tell which executor is active during a long-running step, per OBS-0d4c2311). runHostDelegationObligation — the branch that drives the potentially long-running host-delegation / in-process rolling / NIM hybrid-partition dispatch (materializeReviewRun, driveRollingAuditDispatch, driveWithNoProgressRetry) — never touches that marker, so a host relying on it for liveness during the review-dispatch obligation sees no started_at/status update for the whole duration of that step.
- Evidence: 4 items (top: "src/audit/cli/nextStepHelpers.ts:1418-1424 - executeAndRecord writes steps/deterministic-progress.json with status:"running"/started_at before every determinist…") — see audit-findings.json for the full list

### COR-8c6a7db6 — Linked smoke's present_report handling lacks the friction-triage attestation loop its packaged twin has, so it asserts status 'complete' on a step the CLI emits as 'ready'

The audit orchestrator folds a mandatory friction-triage close-out into the terminal present_report step (it pauses at status 'ready' until the host disposes friction categories — src/audit/orchestrator/nextStep.ts documents this and smoke-packaged-audit-code.mjs handles it by writing category_attestations).

- Severity: medium
- Confidence: medium
- Lens: correctness
- Grounding: grounded
- Files: `scripts/audit/smoke-linked-audit-code.mjs:174–186`, `scripts/audit/smoke-linked-audit-code.mjs:831–833`
- Details: The audit orchestrator folds a mandatory friction-triage close-out into the terminal present_report step (it pauses at status 'ready' until the host disposes friction categories — src/audit/orchestrator/nextStep.ts documents this and smoke-packaged-audit-code.mjs handles it by writing category_attestations). smoke-linked-audit-code.mjs's nextStepUntilPresentReport returns the first present_report step unconditionally and then asserts `presented.status === "complete"`, so a linked smoke run hits the ready-state friction pause and fails the assertion. The two smokes are near-identical ~900-line copies that have already drifted on this behavior.
- Evidence: 4 items (top: "scripts/audit/smoke-linked-audit-code.mjs:174-186 - nextStepUntilPresentReport returns the first present_report step with no friction_record handling.") — see audit-findings.json for the full list

### COR-27f0f33c-2 — Malformed or missing findings array silently scores as "zero findings"

cmdScoreAudit reads audit-findings.json and, if the `findings` field is absent or not an array, silently substitutes an empty array instead of failing loud.

- Severity: medium
- Confidence: medium
- Lens: correctness
- Grounding: grounded
- Files: `src/audit/cli/scoreAuditCommand.ts:51–61`
- Details: cmdScoreAudit reads audit-findings.json and, if the `findings` field is absent or not an array, silently substitutes an empty array instead of failing loud. A truncated or malformed findings file therefore scores as a clean run with zero findings and no diagnostic, which can let a broken input pass the hallucination-rate regression gate that is the command's entire purpose.
- Evidence: 3 items (top: "src/audit/cli/scoreAuditCommand.ts:61 - `findingsDoc.findings` defaults to `[]` with no warning when the field is missing or the wrong shape; there is no consol…") — see audit-findings.json for the full list

### COR-7ee1a2c8 — Mid-flight merge classifies still-running workers as terminal failures and clears their claims

merge-and-ingest has no barrier against being invoked while dispatched workers (timeout_ms up to 30 min) are still running: a task whose result file is not yet on disk is pushed to `failing` as 'Missing audit result for assigned task', its claim is then unconditionally cleared via claimRegistry.clear(terminalTaskIds), and a retry-dispatch is written — so a slow-but-healthy worker is double-dispatched, and its late result can then be excluded as unowned by the ownership gate after a peer re-claims.

- Severity: medium
- Confidence: medium
- Lens: correctness
- Grounding: grounded
- Files: `src/audit/cli/mergeAndIngestCommand.ts:405`, `src/audit/cli/mergeAndIngestCommand.ts:833`
- Evidence: 2 items (top: "src/audit/cli/mergeAndIngestCommand.ts:405 - a missing (possibly just not-yet-written) result is recorded as a terminal failure") — see audit-findings.json for the full list

### MNT-124c4201-2 — Near-1:1 test-to-source ratio includes a growing meta-suite that tests the test suite's own conventions

Raised by the pragmatist perspective.

- Severity: medium
- Confidence: medium
- Lens: maintainability
- Grounding: grounded
- Systemic: yes
- Files: `tests/audit/audit-tests-invariants.test.mjs`, `tests/shared/shared-tests-invariants.test.mjs`, `tests/shared/maintainability-split-rules.test.mjs`
- Details: Raised by the pragmatist perspective. 535 test files against 514 source files, several of which verify repo conventions rather than product behavior (audit-tests-invariants, remediate-tests-invariants, shared-tests-invariants, loop-core-gate-parity, maintainability-split-rules). Meta-tests are a legitimate guardrail in small doses, but at this density every source change pays an implicit 'keep the meta-suite happy' tax unrelated to whether the audit finds real defects. Worth auditing which invariant/meta tests have ever caught a real regression versus existing because the philosophy said they should. JUDGE NOTE: this coexists with, rather than contradicts, DR-031 (e2e gap carried from the prior verdict) — the suite is simultaneously unit/meta-heavy and end-to-end-light, which strengthens the case for rebalancing test investment rather than just adding or removing volume.
- Evidence: 2 items (top: "runtime:unit:tests-audit: not_confirmed — Deterministic runtime command failed with exit code 1: npm test") — see audit-findings.json for the full list

### MNT-b38f2c8a-2 — nextStepHelpers.ts is a 2563-line low-cohesion module, contrary to its own stated purpose

The file's own header says it exists to split concerns out of nextStepCommand.ts so "each module [is] focused on a single concern", but it has grown to 2563 lines covering incoming-artifact quarantine/unwrap logic, 8 distinct host-gate branch handlers, cycle guards, the whole obligation-engine binding (buildAuditObligations), and the host-delegation dispatch orchestration (NIM hybrid split + rolling drive).

- Severity: medium
- Confidence: medium
- Lens: maintainability
- Grounding: grounded
- Files: `src/audit/cli/nextStepHelpers.ts:1–8`
- Details: The file's own header says it exists to split concerns out of nextStepCommand.ts so "each module [is] focused on a single concern", but it has grown to 2563 lines covering incoming-artifact quarantine/unwrap logic, 8 distinct host-gate branch handlers, cycle guards, the whole obligation-engine binding (buildAuditObligations), and the host-delegation dispatch orchestration (NIM hybrid split + rolling drive). The packet's own graph metrics record cohesion=0.2 with 115 boundary edges against only 1 internal edge, consistent with a single file absorbing many only-loosely-related responsibilities that would read and change more safely split by concern (quarantine/unwrap, host-gate branches, obligation binding, cycle guards).
- Evidence: 4 items (top: "src/audit/cli/nextStepHelpers.ts:1-8 - module doc states the extraction goal is single-concern modules.") — see audit-findings.json for the full list

### REL-e362503b — No mid-run re-negotiation when host dispatch capability changes

Carried from the prior verdict (unaffected by the upstream changes).

- Severity: medium
- Confidence: medium
- Lens: reliability
- Grounding: grounded
- Files: `src/remediate/steps/nextStep.ts`, `src/shared/providers/providerConfirmation.ts`
- Details: Carried from the prior verdict (unaffected by the upstream changes). Dispatch depends on host capabilities (subagent support, max-concurrent, model roster) negotiated at startup via handshake; hardcoded fallbacks cover absence, but nothing adapts if capability changes mid-run — reduced concurrency or a deprecated roster model leaves the run on stale capability or forces failure. Add a periodic re-probe (e.g. every N rounds) to refresh capabilities and adapt dispatch strategy when the host context changes.
- Evidence: 3 items (top: "runtime:unit:src-remediate: not_confirmed — Deterministic runtime command failed with exit code 1: npm test") — see audit-findings.json for the full list

### CFG-157221a7 — Packaged smoke installs the tarball without redirecting HOME, so the package postinstall targets the operator's real global config (or goes silently untested)

smoke-packaged-audit-code.mjs installs the packed tarball with createIsolatedNpmEnv(), which strips npm_config_*/tokens but preserves the real HOME/USERPROFILE.

- Severity: medium
- Confidence: medium
- Lens: config_deployment
- Grounding: grounded
- Files: `scripts/audit/smoke-packaged-audit-code.mjs:626–649`, `scripts/audit/smoke-packaged-audit-code.mjs:478–496`, `scripts/remediate/smoke-packaged-remediate-code.mjs:153–161`
- Details: smoke-packaged-audit-code.mjs installs the packed tarball with createIsolatedNpmEnv(), which strips npm_config_*/tokens but preserves the real HOME/USERPROFILE. When npm runs the dependency postinstall (older npm, or any config allowing scripts), the package's postinstall deploys host assets into the operator's actual ~/.claude, ~/.codex and OpenCode global config as a side effect of a release-gate smoke; when npm 12+ blocks dependency scripts, the postinstall path is silently untested instead. The remediate packaged smoke handles both halves: it redirects HOME/USERPROFILE to a fakeHome and explicitly enables scripts.
- Evidence: 5 items (top: "scripts/audit/smoke-packaged-audit-code.mjs:478-496 - createIsolatedNpmEnv copies process.env (minus npm_config_*/tokens) and never overrides HOME/USERPROFILE.") — see audit-findings.json for the full list

### PRF-6fa9de59 — Parallelize independent test suites across separate vitest processes

vitest.config.ts runs 515 tests (246 audit + 138 remediate + 131 shared) sequentially in a single process.

- Severity: medium
- Confidence: medium
- Lens: performance
- Grounding: grounded
- Systemic: yes
- Files: `vitest.config.ts`, `package.json`, `tests/audit`, `tests/remediate` +1 more
- Details: vitest.config.ts runs 515 tests (246 audit + 138 remediate + 131 shared) sequentially in a single process. The three test suites are independent (no shared mutable state, distinct setup/teardown, separate fixture dirs). Parallelize via separate vitest processes or a test orchestration layer (e.g., npm scripts running vitest run tests/audit, vitest run tests/remediate, vitest run tests/shared concurrently). Requires verification that fixture dirs and temp state don't conflict, but test isolation appears safe. Estimated wall-time reduction: ~40-50% (from 3×sequential to max(audit_time, remediate_time, shared_time)).
- Evidence: runtime:flow:flow:host:npm-release-publish: not_confirmed — Deterministic runtime command failed with exit code 1: npm test

### COR-c9dd2a3c — readActiveDispatch swallows ALL read errors (not just missing-file), silently defeating the pause/livelock counter

readActiveDispatch (used by every persist/clear/advance helper in this sibling dispatch/pausePersist.ts module) catches every error from readJsonFile — corrupt JSON, EPERM, etc, not only a missing file — and returns null; advanceHostDispatchPause then treats that as 'no prior pause' and resets pause_count to 0, so one transient read glitch can silently defeat the livelock guard, and persistPausedState no-ops instead of ever writing the pause to disk.

- Severity: medium
- Confidence: medium
- Lens: correctness
- Grounding: grounded
- Files: `src/audit/cli/dispatch/pausePersist.ts:23–30`, `src/audit/cli/dispatch/pausePersist.ts:39–40`, `src/audit/cli/dispatch/pausePersist.ts:167–175`
- Evidence: 4 items (top: "src/audit/cli/dispatch/pausePersist.ts:28 - readActiveDispatch's .catch(() => null) is unconditional, unlike the isFileMissingError-gated pattern used for the s…") — see audit-findings.json for the full list

### COR-0cb408b9 — Resume transition returns status 'partial', which the module's own contract defines as livelock-terminal

advanceRollingPause returns undefined in TWO distinct cases: the run RESUMED (next.kind === 'running' — pause cleared, no terminal recorded, packets should re-dispatch next pass) and the run went livelock-TERMINAL (partial-completion terminal recorded).

- Severity: medium
- Confidence: medium
- Lens: correctness
- Grounding: grounded
- Files: `src/audit/cli/rollingAuditDispatch.ts:543–553`, `src/audit/cli/rollingAuditDispatch.ts:657–661`
- Details: advanceRollingPause returns undefined in TWO distinct cases: the run RESUMED (next.kind === 'running' — pause cleared, no terminal recorded, packets should re-dispatch next pass) and the run went livelock-TERMINAL (partial-completion terminal recorded). driveRollingAuditDispatch collapses both into `status: pausedState ? "paused" : run.status`, so a resume-eligible pass surfaces as status 'partial' with non-empty stranded_ids — a status the DriveRollingAuditDispatchResult doc explicitly defines as 'terminal: the pause limit was reached (livelock) and the stranded packets are yielded to synthesis on partial coverage'. A caller keying handoff rendering or terminal handling off this status will misreport a resumable run as a give-up (no terminal actually exists on disk, so state derivation and the return value disagree).
- Evidence: 4 items (top: "src/audit/cli/rollingAuditDispatch.ts:133-137 - the result contract: 'partial — terminal: the pause limit was reached (livelock)'.") — see audit-findings.json for the full list

### ARC-d45f1dcd — Risk score is an unbounded additive sum hard-clamped to 10, so a third of units tie at the ceiling where ranking matters most

Raised by the mathematician perspective; clamp verified at src/audit/extractors/risk.ts:202 (`Math.min(MAX_RISK_SCORE, riskScore)`, MAX_RISK_SCORE=10).

- Severity: medium
- Confidence: medium
- Lens: architecture
- Grounding: grounded
- Systemic: yes
- Files: `src/audit/extractors/risk.ts`
- Details: Raised by the mathematician perspective; clamp verified at src/audit/extractors/risk.ts:202 (`Math.min(MAX_RISK_SCORE, riskScore)`, MAX_RISK_SCORE=10). risk_score sums several independently-uncapped terms (unit risk, flow hits, external hits, stateful-behavior bonus, the capped structural family, complexity/duplication bonuses) then applies one hard ceiling; in the current run 8 of 22 scored units (36%) land at exactly 10 despite visibly different signal profiles, collapsing the ranking precisely at the top where dispatch prioritization, review-packet ordering, and human triage need discrimination. The file itself shows the author knows correlated signals need bounding (STRUCTURAL_FAMILY_CAP exists so one family 'cannot ALONE saturate risk_score') — the same discipline was not applied to the outer sum. Low-cost fixes: persist the pre-clamp raw score alongside the clamped one so downstream consumers can break ceiling ties, or rescale weights so 10 is reached only by genuinely maximal units. Note: per DR-001, the member_of_cycle input to this score is currently contaminated — fix that first, then re-evaluate the distribution.
- Evidence: runtime:unit:src-audit: not_confirmed — Deterministic runtime command failed with exit code 1: npm test

### TST-efea49c2 — seam-obligation-abstraction.test.ts is a cross-package parity test standing in for a missing single-sourced schema

The file's own docstring states its purpose is to fail if the shared `buildObligationLedger` producer and the remediate-code `validateObligationLedger` consumer diverge on the obligation-ledger contract shape.

- Confidence: medium
- Lens: tests
- Grounding: grounded

- Details: The file's own docstring states its purpose is to fail if the shared `buildObligationLedger` producer and the remediate-code `validateObligationLedger` consumer diverge on the obligation-ledger contract shape. That is a drift-guard test: it exists only because the required-field/enum rules (id, kind, depends_on, status, contract_version) are hand-duplicated on both sides rather than derived from one schema. The real defect the test is compensating for is the missing shared schema; if the guard is ever deleted or skipped, the two sides can silently diverge with no other test catching it.

### PRF-e082a92f — acquireLock issues mkdir(recursive) on every acquisition, adding an FS round-trip to the hot admission path

Every lock acquisition unconditionally awaits mkdir(dirname(lockPath), {recursive:true}) before the wx create.

- Severity: low
- Confidence: high
- Lens: performance
- Grounding: grounded
- Files: `src/shared/quota/fileLock.ts:147–152`
- Details: Every lock acquisition unconditionally awaits mkdir(dirname(lockPath), {recursive:true}) before the wx create. The reservation ledger takes this lock for EVERY admit, reconcile, outstanding, snapshot and reclaim call (one admit + one reconcile per dispatched packet), and quota-state mutations ride the same primitive, so a run of N packets pays ~2N+ redundant mkdir syscalls for a directory that exists after the first call. Cheap individually but pure overhead in the per-packet admission loop; a once-per-path memo (or creating on ENOENT only) removes it.
- Evidence: 5 items (top: "src/shared/quota/fileLock.ts:151 - the mkdir runs unconditionally on every acquireLock call, before the create attempt.") — see audit-findings.json for the full list

### COR-cdfcb034 — checkAffectedFileIntegrity's ENOENT-to-missing branch is unreachable; a file deleted mid-check is misclassified as an io_error

The hash callback catches ENOENT to return { kind: 'missing' }, but hashAffectedPath can never throw: it wraps its stat/hash work in its own try/catch that swallows every error (reportHashIoError + return undefined), and returns undefined for a non-existent path via its own existsSync guard.

- Confidence: high
- Lens: correctness

So a file deleted between the shared helper's exists() check and the hash call yields undefined and is bucketed as an io_error instead of missing, and the catch/ENOENT branch is dead code.
- Evidence: 4 items (top: "src/remediate/utils/fileIntegrity.ts:132-145 - hashAffectedPath returns undefined for both a vanished file (existsSync false) and a real read failure; it never …") — see audit-findings.json for the full list

### MNT-07e1d9a0-2 — Dated 'sunset cleanup' deprecation has no enforcement and has already lapsed

Raised by the adversary perspective.

- Severity: low
- Confidence: high
- Lens: maintainability
- Grounding: grounded
- Files: `src/shared/providers/agyProvider.ts`
- Details: Raised by the adversary perspective. src/shared/providers/agyProvider.ts falls back from the `agy` CLI to a legacy `gemini` binary with a comment gating it 'for July 18, 2026 sunset cleanup' — a dated commitment with no code that reads a clock, warns, or disables after the date, which has now passed (2026-07-21) with the fallback fully live and no runtime deprecation signal when the gemini path is taken. Small but concrete instance of a class worth guarding: a dated cleanup promise living only in a comment is mechanically indistinguishable from one that will never be honored. Either add a runtime lapsed-date warning/removal, or drop the date; consider a repo-wide lint for dated TODO/sunset markers past their date.
- Evidence: runtime:unit:src-shared: not_confirmed — Deterministic runtime command failed with exit code 1: npm test

### OBS-da2c06a4 — Entrypoint error handler discards stack, cause, and error class — only the message survives

- Details: readLedger silently filters out any lease that fails the isLease shape check ('junk is dropped silently'). Since a dropped lease means a live reservation stops counting — the fail-open direction is over-admission — the event is operationally meaningful, yet nothing records how many leases were discarded or that the ledger was found corrupt, so an operator diagnosing an overshoot has no trace that reservations were being lost.

### TST-dfaecc8c — Meta-tests in shared-tests-invariants.test.mjs are drift guards for export stability

- Details: Tests in shared-tests-invariants.test.mjs (INV-shared-tests-07 group, lines 215-300+) enumerate and check the presence of exported symbols from shared/index.ts. ### PRF-e045499d — Run the two independent postinstall sub-deployers concurrently instead of serially blocking

scripts/postinstall.mjs deploys BOTH host asset sets (audit-code and remediate-code) by looping over ["./audit/postinstall.mjs", "./remediate/postinstall.mjs"] and calling `spawnSync` on each in turn — the second sub-deployer does not start until the first has fully exited, even though the script's own comment says each sub-deployer is 'self-contained' and they are run 'as isolated child processes so one host's deployment never corrupts the other's.' That isolation rationale argues FOR concurrency, not against it — nothing links the two runs except the shared parent waiting on them one at a time.

- Severity: low
- Confidence: high
- Lens: performance
- Grounding: grounded
- Systemic: yes
- Files: `scripts/postinstall.mjs`
- Details: scripts/postinstall.mjs deploys BOTH host asset sets (audit-code and remediate-code) by looping over ["./audit/postinstall.mjs", "./remediate/postinstall.mjs"] and calling `spawnSync` on each in turn — the second sub-deployer does not start until the first has fully exited, even though the script's own comment says each sub-deployer is 'self-contained' and they are run 'as isolated child processes so one host's deployment never corrupts the other's.' That isolation rationale argues FOR concurrency, not against it — nothing links the two runs except the shared parent waiting on them one at a time. Since they are already isolated, independent child processes with no shared mutable state (this is exactly the 'serial that could be parallel' case), replace the sequential spawnSync loop with a concurrent `spawn` + `Promise.all` (or `child_process.fork`/async spawnSync-equivalent) so both host installs run in parallel and postinstall wall time drops to max(audit_install, remediate_install) instead of their sum, directly speeding up every `npm install` of the package. This is a distinct improvement from the already-banked postinstall-duplication finding, which targets code-structure duplication between scripts/audit/postinstall.mjs and scripts/remediate/postinstall.mjs, not the root orchestrator's serial execution model.
- Evidence: runtime:flow:flow:host:wrapper-bootstrap-install: not_confirmed — Deterministic runtime command failed with exit code 1: npm test

### COR-33145bd1 — runDistCommandInline builds the dist file URL by string concatenation instead of pathToFileURL

The mcp inline path constructs a file URL as 'file:///' + path with backslashes swapped.

- Severity: low
- Confidence: high

- Grounding: grounded
- Files: `wrapper/audit-code-wrapper-lib.mjs:273–276`
- Details: The mcp inline path constructs a file URL as 'file:///' + path with backslashes swapped. On POSIX (absolute paths start with '/') this yields file:////... (empty authority + double-slash path), and any checkout path containing URL-significant characters ('%', '#', '?') is misparsed because nothing is percent-encoded. node:url's pathToFileURL exists precisely for this and is already imported adjacent (fileURLToPath is used at line 4/22).
- Evidence: 3 items (top: "wrapper/audit-code-wrapper-lib.mjs:274 - manual 'file:///' concatenation: on non-Windows platforms distCliEntry already begins with '/', producing 'file:////...…") — see audit-findings.json for the full list

### DAT-c5ea3938 — Snapshot entries are cast, not validated: malformed field types flow through to every consumer

loadTable accepts any object value as a ModelStatics entry via a bare cast (`value as ModelStatics`), and the __by_provider index is cast wholesale.

- Severity: low
- Confidence: high
- Lens: data_integrity
- Grounding: grounded
- Files: `src/shared/quota/modelStatics.ts:70–82`
- Details: loadTable accepts any object value as a ModelStatics entry via a bare cast (`value as ModelStatics`), and the __by_provider index is cast wholesale. Field types (context_tokens/output_tokens numbers, price.input/output numbers) are never checked at this trust boundary — the file is regenerated by an external script from a community dataset — so a snapshot with, e.g., stringified numbers or a nested wrong shape is served as-is, and each consumer must independently re-guard (resolveLimits does; any price consumer that reads statics.price without a typeof check would ingest garbage).
- Evidence: 3 items (top: "src/shared/quota/modelStatics.ts:73-75 - the __by_provider block is cast to Record<string, StaticsTable> with no per-entry shape check.") — see audit-findings.json for the full list

### REL-c5ea3938 — Sticky failure cache: one transient read failure permanently forfeits the statics dataset for the process lifetime, silently

loadTable caches null on ANY failure and never retries (cache !== undefined short-circuits).
