- Confidence: high
- Lens: reliability
- Grounding: grounded
- Files: `src/shared/quota/modelStatics.ts:59–87`
- Details: loadTable caches null on ANY failure and never retries (cache !== undefined short-circuits). A transient first-lookup failure (e.g. EBUSY/EPERM while the build's copy-data-assets step or an AV scan briefly holds dist/shared/data on Windows — a failure class this repo's own conventions flag as common) permanently degrades every subsequent resolveModelStatics call to undefined for the whole process, with no log or diagnostic, so limit resolution silently falls to the conservative flat default even though the dataset is readable moments later.

### COR-756614b5-2 — Write-scope enforcement happens at merge time, not at worker dispatch; wastes effort

Carried from the prior verdict (unaffected by the upstream changes).

- Severity: low
- Confidence: high
- Lens: correctness
- Grounding: grounded
- Files: `src/remediate/steps/dispatch/acceptNode.ts`, `src/remediate/steps/dispatch/writeScope.ts`
- Details: Carried from the prior verdict (unaffected by the upstream changes). The write-scope gate runs AFTER the worker completes and the node branch is committed; out-of-scope edits are safely rejected at merge, but the worker already spent time and tokens producing them. A pre-dispatch scope injection into the worker prompt plus an early post-response check would catch and correct scope errors before the full accept cycle. Current design is correct but non-optimal.
- Evidence: runtime:unit:src-remediate: not_confirmed — Deterministic runtime command failed with exit code 1: npm test

Both the audit and remediate io modules share the behavior.

- Evidence: 4 items (top: "scripts/audit/smoke-packaged-audit-code.mjs:603-624 - `npm pack --json` runs with lifecycle scripts enabled and the stdout is fed straight into JSON.parse.") — see audit-findings.json for the full list

### COR-97fb1629 — Bare dist CLI invocation silently defaults to sample-run, contradicting the wrapper's no-implicit-default contract

main() defaults a missing command to 'sample-run', so invoking dist/cli.js (or dist/audit/index.js) with no arguments silently starts a state-mutating sample audit run instead of printing usage.

- Severity: low
- Confidence: medium
- Lens: correctness
- Grounding: grounded
- Files: `src/audit/cli.ts:62–68`
- Details: main() defaults a missing command to 'sample-run', so invoking dist/cli.js (or dist/audit/index.js) with no arguments silently starts a state-mutating sample audit run instead of printing usage. The wrapper explicitly encodes the opposite contract ('No implicit default command: ... A bare invocation prints usage', wrapper/audit-code-wrapper-lib.mjs:350-356), so the two entrypoints disagree on what a bare invocation does; anyone bypassing the wrapper (dev, CI, a host permission rule matching the dist path) gets the surprising behavior.
- Evidence: 3 items (top: "src/audit/cli.ts:64 - argv[2] ?? "sample-run" makes sample-run the implicit default for a bare invocation.") — see audit-findings.json for the full list

### TST-f324e33c — build.mjs's independent resolveSpawn quoting copy is outside the parity guard and appears untested

The parity test pins only wrapper-lib's copy of the cmd quoting contract.

- Severity: low
- Confidence: medium
- Lens: tests
- Grounding: grounded
- Files: `wrapper/audit-code-wrapper-build.mjs:21–33`
- Details: The parity test pins only wrapper-lib's copy of the cmd quoting contract. build.mjs carries its own inline resolveSpawn with materially weaker quoting (no metachar caret-escape, no '%' rejection) and exports no hook for the parity test — so the one copy that has actually drifted from the hardened contract is exactly the copy no test observes. If the duplication is not eliminated (see TST-W1-001), the guard should at minimum cover every copy.
- Evidence: 3 items (top: "wrapper/audit-code-wrapper-build.mjs:21-33 - a private, non-exported resolveSpawn implementing the same win32 shim contract with weaker escaping; being non-expo…") — see audit-findings.json for the full list

### COR-3acd075e — buildTaskSections silently defaults a missing total_lines to 0 instead of failing loud

coverageTemplate in buildTaskSections falls back to a bare 0 when a task's own file_line_counts is missing the path AND the aggregate lineIndex also misses it (e.g.

- Severity: low
- Confidence: medium
- Lens: correctness
- Grounding: grounded
- Files: `src/audit/cli/dispatch/packetPrompt.ts:239–242`, `src/audit/cli/dispatch/packetPrompt.ts:139–140`
- Details: coverageTemplate in buildTaskSections falls back to a bare 0 when a task's own file_line_counts is missing the path AND the aggregate lineIndex also misses it (e.g. a synthetic follow-up/deepening task without file_line_counts, for a file no other pending task currently covers). The emitted packet prompt explicitly warns the worker that a zero/omitted total_lines 'will cause fatal validation errors', yet the tool itself can embed a silent 0 into the copy-exactly coverage template with no warning surfaced, contradicting its own documented contract. The sibling extractPacketAnchor function in the same file avoids this by falling back to the packet's own total_lines instead of 0, showing the safer pattern already exists nearby but isn't reused here.
- Evidence: 4 items (top: "src/audit/cli/dispatch/packetPrompt.ts:241 - coverageTemplate computes total_lines via `task.file_line_counts?.[path] ?? lineIndex[path] ?? 0`, silently landing…") — see audit-findings.json for the full list

### REL-5398b45a — cancel-in-progress on main pushes leaves intermediate main commits without a completed suite run

The concurrency group is keyed on github.ref with cancel-in-progress: true, so on branch main two pushes in quick succession cancel the earlier commit's in-flight matrix.

- Severity: low
- Confidence: medium
- Lens: reliability
- Grounding: grounded
- Files: `.github/workflows/audit-code-test-suite.yml:41–43`
- Details: The concurrency group is keyed on github.ref with cancel-in-progress: true, so on branch main two pushes in quick succession cancel the earlier commit's in-flight matrix. Combined with the release-bump skip, stretches of main history can carry no completed orchestration-suite evidence, which weakens bisection and the 'green main' signal. This is a common intentional trade for PR branches; on the trunk it silently drops coverage of superseded commits.
- Evidence: .github/workflows/audit-code-test-suite.yml:41-43 - `group: audit-code-test-suite-${{ github.workflow }}-${{ github.ref }}` with cancel-in-progress applies to refs/heads/main pushes as well as PRs, so a newer main push cancels the previous main commit's run.

### REL-8c3956e7 — claude-worker silently substitutes the dummy auth token when the configured api_key_env variable is unset or blank

- Confidence: medium

- Details: When config.api_key_env names an env var that is unset or empty at launch, the provider falls back to CLAUDE_WORKER_DUMMY_API_KEY with no warning. Against a keyed proxy every spawned worker then fails auth, and the failure surfaces only as opaque per-packet worker errors — there is no diagnostic tying the failures to the missing credential, so the misconfiguration is expensive to trace.

### OBS-14ae4e12 — ClaudeWorkerProvider's per-launch config-dir cleanup failure is swallowed with zero diagnostic signal

If cleanup keeps failing (e.g. AV lock, permissions), temp dirs under os.tmpdir()/audit-tools-claude-worker accumulate on every dispatch with no observable trace pointing at the cause, unlike comparable best-effort paths elsewhere in the packet (e.g. spawnLoggedCommand's onLogStreamError, which annotates and surfaces stream failures).

### TST-e36d2490 — Concurrency-peak assertions rely on a fixed 5ms setTimeout to force interleaving instead of a deterministic synchronization signal

Both rolling-dispatch-file-ownership-ordering.test.ts and rolling-scheduler.test.ts measure 'peak in-flight concurrency' / 'max concurrent rebuilds' by having each dispatched node/rebuild `await new Promise((r) => setTimeout(r, 5))` before completing, then reading a counter.

- Severity: low
- Confidence: medium
- Lens: tests
- Grounding: grounded
- Files: `tests/remediate/rolling-dispatch-file-ownership-ordering.test.ts`, `tests/remediate/rolling-scheduler.test.ts`
- Details: Both rolling-dispatch-file-ownership-ordering.test.ts and rolling-scheduler.test.ts measure 'peak in-flight concurrency' / 'max concurrent rebuilds' by having each dispatched node/rebuild `await new Promise((r) => setTimeout(r, 5))` before completing, then reading a counter. This assumes every peer node in a sub-wave is admitted and starts incrementing its counter within that 5ms window before the first one resolves; on a loaded CI runner or a slow machine a scheduler that starts nodes with any meaningful stagger could make the peak assertion (e.g. `expect(peak).toBe(3)`) intermittently under-report, since the test infers concurrency from wall-clock timing rather than an explicit admission barrier.
- Evidence: 3 items (top: "tests/remediate/rolling-dispatch-file-ownership-ordering.test.ts:206 - the run() helper increments/decrements `inFlight` around a 5ms sleep and the test then as…") — see audit-findings.json for the full list

### OPR-98793a71 — Consolidate CLI command dispatch pattern

src/audit/cli/ contains 49 command files (advanceAuditCommand, analyzerInstallCommand, ...).

- Severity: low
- Confidence: medium
- Lens: operability
- Grounding: grounded
- Systemic: yes
- Files: `src/audit/cli`, `src/audit/cli.ts`
- Details: src/audit/cli/ contains 49 command files (advanceAuditCommand, analyzerInstallCommand, ...). Each typically follows a pattern: parse args → load artifact bundle → execute logic → write step. The command dispatch in cli.ts (the entry point) maps command names to these files. Consider: (1) parameterizing the command registration so new commands don't require a new file + dispatch entry; (2) extracting a `CommandContext` type that all commands receive (artifacts, config, etc.) to reduce boilerplate; (3) consolidating logically-grouped commands (all validation commands, all synthesis commands) into shared modules with sub-dispatch. Not urgent, but high-friction on adding new commands.
- Evidence: runtime:unit:src-audit: not_confirmed — Deterministic runtime command failed with exit code 1: npm test

### COR-9a886162 — Corrupt active-dispatch.json is silently treated as absent, dropping resumable pause state

readActiveDispatch (and the parallel inline read in dispatch.ts) swallows ALL read errors via .catch(() => null), including the 'Invalid JSON' error readJsonFile throws on a corrupt file.

- Severity: low
- Confidence: medium
- Lens: correctness
- Grounding: grounded
- Files: `src/audit/cli/dispatch/pausePersist.ts:24–31`, `src/audit/cli/dispatch.ts:679–681`
- Details: readActiveDispatch (and the parallel inline read in dispatch.ts) swallows ALL read errors via .catch(() => null), including the 'Invalid JSON' error readJsonFile throws on a corrupt file. A corrupt active-dispatch.json is indistinguishable from an absent one: persistPausedState silently no-ops, the carried paused_state (pause_count, settled_exclusions) is lost, and the livelock counter restarts from zero — extending a stall the livelock bound exists to end, with no diagnostic anywhere.
- Evidence: 4 items (top: "src/audit/cli/dispatch/pausePersist.ts:29 - .catch(() => null) collapses ENOENT, JSON-parse failure, and IO errors into the same 'no artifact' answer; persistPa…") — see audit-findings.json for the full list

### TST-ba62a837 — Cycle-guard fixture bakes in a hand-derived internal counting formula

makeCycleTrigger()'s docstring hand-derives the exact internal arithmetic the finalization cycle guard uses to decide when to fire ('index + 1 - seenStateSignatures.size >= tolerance') and bakes that formula into the fixture instead of driving the guard to trip through its public/observable behavior.

- Severity: low
- Confidence: medium
- Lens: tests
- Grounding: grounded
- Files: `tests/audit/finalization-cycle-guard.test.mjs:96–122`
- Details: makeCycleTrigger()'s docstring hand-derives the exact internal arithmetic the finalization cycle guard uses to decide when to fire ('index + 1 - seenStateSignatures.size >= tolerance') and bakes that formula into the fixture instead of driving the guard to trip through its public/observable behavior. If the internal accounting is ever refactored without changing observable behavior, this fixture's hand-derived index/seed values can silently stop tripping the guard (a false negative) rather than the test failing loudly.
- Evidence: 2 items (top: "tests/audit/finalization-cycle-guard.test.mjs:100-121 - the fixture builder's comment shows manual algebra re-deriving the exact trip condition of checkFinaliza…") — see audit-findings.json for the full list

### OBS-bed27a7c — Directory-hash I/O errors are reported at the top-level path, never the actual failing child file

hashDirectorySync/hashDirectory's recursive walk reads each child file with no per-file try/catch of its own; a mid-walk failure (permission denied, file removed) propagates up and is only caught in hashAffectedPathSync/hashAffectedPath, which logs via reportHashIoError(absolutePath, err) using the top-level directory path passed in, not the specific child file that actually failed — so the emitted file_integrity_io_error diagnostic never identifies which file inside the directory caused the failure.

- Severity: low
- Confidence: medium
- Lens: observability
- Grounding: grounded
- Files: `src/remediate/utils/fileIntegrity.ts:68–87`, `src/remediate/utils/fileIntegrity.ts:114–129`
- Evidence: 4 items (top: "src/remediate/utils/fileIntegrity.ts:71-83 - hashDirectorySync's inner visit() calls readFileSync(child) directly with no local try/catch, so a failure on any n…") — see audit-findings.json for the full list

### COR-a8f418c2 — dispatch-status undercounts total/completed tasks whose results predate the current run

cmdDispatchStatus skips every dispatch-result-map entry whose packet_id is '__prior_dispatch__' before building packetStatus, so those tasks (results that already existed on disk before this run started) never contribute to total_tasks or completed_tasks — the reported progress undercounts true scope on any resumed run.

- Severity: low
- Confidence: medium
- Lens: correctness
- Grounding: grounded
- Files: `src/audit/cli/dispatchStatusCommand.ts:40`, `src/audit/cli/dispatchStatusCommand.ts:63`
- Evidence: 3 items (top: "src/audit/cli/dispatchStatusCommand.ts:40 - entries with packet_id '__prior_dispatch__' are skipped and never pushed into packetStatus.") — see audit-findings.json for the full list

### MNT-649fbe02 — Duplicated contract/conceptual apply blocks in handleDesignReviewBranch

handleDesignReviewBranch applies the contract and conceptual design-review results with two near-identical blocks (quarantine-record / ok-merge-with-groundDesignFindings-and-mark-reviewed / filter-out-prior-rejection), differing only in which `existing.*_findings`/`*_reviewed` field and rejection-pass literal ("contract" vs "conceptual") they touch.

- Severity: low
- Confidence: medium
- Lens: maintainability
- Grounding: grounded
- Files: `src/audit/cli/nextStepHelpers.ts:905–914`, `src/audit/cli/nextStepHelpers.ts:916–925`
- Details: handleDesignReviewBranch applies the contract and conceptual design-review results with two near-identical blocks (quarantine-record / ok-merge-with-groundDesignFindings-and-mark-reviewed / filter-out-prior-rejection), differing only in which `existing.*_findings`/`*_reviewed` field and rejection-pass literal ("contract" vs "conceptual") they touch. The comment above the function explicitly documents this branch as intentionally NOT going through the shared `runOmittableGate` engine because its shape "genuinely deviates", but that justification is about the branch's overall control flow, not about these two inner apply blocks, which could still be factored into one small helper parameterized by pass name to avoid a same-edit-in-two-places tell for any future change to how a pass's results are merged.
- Evidence: 3 items (top: "src/audit/cli/nextStepHelpers.ts:905-914 - contract apply block.") — see audit-findings.json for the full list

### PRF-c4b7bd17 — Enable TypeScript incremental compilation so the commit-gate typecheck and the build compile don't each re-check the whole program cold

tsconfig.base.json (extended by tsconfig.json) sets strict/target/module options but no `incremental`/`tsBuildInfoFile`.

- Severity: low
- Confidence: medium
- Lens: performance
- Grounding: grounded
- Systemic: yes
- Files: `tsconfig.base.json`, `tsconfig.json`, `.claude/hooks/pre-commit-gate.mjs`, `package.json`
- Details: tsconfig.base.json (extended by tsconfig.json) sets strict/target/module options but no `incremental`/`tsBuildInfoFile`. Two separate full-program tsc invocations exist over the same `src` tree with nothing shared between them: `npm run build` (`tsc -p tsconfig.json`, emits dist/) and `npm run check` (`tsc -p tsconfig.json --noEmit`), the latter run on EVERY commit by the PreToolUse hook (.claude/hooks/pre-commit-gate.mjs, `execSync('npm run check', ...)`, materialized against a freshly checked-out scratch git index each time — a cold worktree with no carried-over build state). Because neither invocation sets `incremental`, every `npm run check` re-type-checks the entire program from scratch even when only a handful of files changed since the last commit's `build`/`check` pass, and `npm run build` (invoked by `npm test`, `verify:checks`, and every release) likewise starts cold every time. TypeScript's incremental mode exists exactly for this repeated-full-recompile pattern: adding `"incremental": true` plus a `tsBuildInfoFile` (e.g. `.tsbuildinfo`, gitignored) lets a second tsc invocation over largely-unchanged source reuse the cached program/type-check state and only re-verify what changed, instead of a full cold pass. This doesn't remove a step (build and check remain distinct, intentional gates per CLAUDE.md's green-at-every-commit invariant) but cuts the wall-clock cost of the pattern CLAUDE.md's own pipeline-profiling section already names as a tracked cost ('the check/build double-tsc... timed') — this finding is the concrete fix (incremental caching) for a cost the profiling infra measures but nothing yet addresses.
- Evidence: runtime:flow:flow:host:npm-release-publish: not_confirmed — Deterministic runtime command failed with exit code 1: npm test

### COR-811eb3a4 — explain-task aggregates every historical audit_results entry for a task_id, not just the current lineage result

cmdExplainTask filters bundle.audit_results by task_id with no current-lineage filter (the codebase has selectCurrentResults for exactly this purpose), so a task that was re-dispatched or selectively deepened will report matching_result_count / matching_finding_ids that include findings from a superseded prior result alongside the current one.

### COR-948df0a4 — foldAccountCooldown is documented 'Pure' but fabricates updated_at from the ambient clock for synthesized entries

foldAccountCooldown's docstring claims 'Pure; never mutates its inputs', but when ownEntry is null and a sibling contributes a cooldown signal it synthesizes an entry stamped `updated_at: new Date().toISOString()` — a nondeterministic ambient-clock read.

- Severity: low
- Confidence: medium
- Lens: correctness
- Grounding: grounded
- Files: `src/shared/quota/accountId.ts:129–163`
- Details: foldAccountCooldown's docstring claims 'Pure; never mutates its inputs', but when ownEntry is null and a sibling contributes a cooldown signal it synthesizes an entry stamped `updated_at: new Date().toISOString()` — a nondeterministic ambient-clock read. The fabricated timestamp marks data that was actually learned earlier (from a sibling's entry) as freshly captured; QuotaStateEntry.updated_at is consumed as a capture/freshness timestamp elsewhere (learnedQuotaSource maps it to captured_at), so a folded entry can masquerade stale sibling state as fresh. It also makes the fold non-referentially-transparent, defeating the identity comparison at apiPool.foldAccountCooldownAcrossPools (`folded === (pool.quotaStateEntry ?? null)`) which can only ever be true in the all-null case.
- Evidence: 4 items (top: "src/shared/quota/accountId.ts:129 - docstring states 'Pure; never mutates its inputs.' while line 163 reads the ambient clock on the ownEntry-null path.") — see audit-findings.json for the full list

The four 'no-drift: committed X equals a fresh render of the canonical body' tests each re-implement the identical readFileSync + lf()-normalize + expect(...).toBe(lf(rendered)) pattern for a different committed asset path, instead of sharing one assertNoDrift(path, expected, message) helper.

- Severity: low
- Confidence: medium
- Lens: maintainability
- Grounding: grounded
- Files: `tests/audit/host-asset-renderer-drift.test.mjs:100–108`, `tests/audit/host-asset-renderer-drift.test.mjs:110–118`, `tests/audit/host-asset-renderer-drift.test.mjs:128–136`, `tests/audit/host-asset-renderer-drift.test.mjs:142–153`
- Details: The four 'no-drift: committed X equals a fresh render of the canonical body' tests each re-implement the identical readFileSync + lf()-normalize + expect(...).toBe(lf(rendered)) pattern for a different committed asset path, instead of sharing one assertNoDrift(path, expected, message) helper. Any change to the comparison/failure-message logic (e.g. a friendlier diff on mismatch) has to be hand-applied in all four places.
- Evidence: 5 items (top: "tests/audit/host-asset-renderer-drift.test.mjs:100-108 - first no-drift test: const committed = lf(readFileSync(join(repoRoot, ...), "utf8")); expect(committed,…") — see audit-findings.json for the full list

### MNT-4459b0e6 — Hand-maintained SHARED_QUOTA_SYMBOLS / auditor-only rosters duplicate the shared export surface

The parity suite hard-codes three symbol rosters (SHARED_QUOTA_SYMBOLS, plus two auditorOnly lists) that mirror the actual export surfaces of audit-tools/shared quota and the two orchestrators' quota barrels.

- Severity: low
- Confidence: medium
- Lens: maintainability
- Grounding: grounded
- Files: `tests/audit/seam-provider-quota-instantiation-parity.test.mjs:72–111`
- Details: The parity suite hard-codes three symbol rosters (SHARED_QUOTA_SYMBOLS, plus two auditorOnly lists) that mirror the actual export surfaces of audit-tools/shared quota and the two orchestrators' quota barrels. Every export added or removed in shared requires a matching edit here, and a symbol added to shared but not appended to the list is simply never checked, so the guard only decays. Deriving the expected set from the shared module's own export names (Object.keys of the imported namespace, minus a small documented exclusion list) would remove the N-places edit cost.
- Evidence: 3 items (top: "tests/audit/seam-provider-quota-instantiation-parity.test.mjs:72 - ~40-entry hand list of shared quota symbols") — see audit-findings.json for the full list

All three copies capture chunks, restore the original write function in a finally/afterward, and differ only in incidental detail — the same output-capture contract kept in sync by convention rather than one shared test helper (the fix pattern already applied for fileLock's helpers in tests/shared/fileLockTestSupport.mjs).
- Evidence: 5 items (top: "tests/audit/submit-packet-command.test.mjs:134-149 - runSubmit() overrides process.stdout.write/process.stderr.write/console.log, accumulates into local vars, r…") — see audit-findings.json for the full list

### TST-7f450d51 — hostAssets.ts splitFrontmatter duplicates scripts/shared/install-host-assets.mjs with no direct unit test and no drift guard

The TS splitFrontmatter (consumed by `remediate-code ensure`) is a deliberate second copy of the frontmatter-stripping logic in scripts/shared/install-host-assets.mjs (the postinstall script cannot import TS).

- Severity: low
- Confidence: medium
- Lens: tests
- Grounding: grounded
- Files: `src/remediate/utils/hostAssets.ts:4–15`
- Details: The TS splitFrontmatter (consumed by `remediate-code ensure`) is a deliberate second copy of the frontmatter-stripping logic in scripts/shared/install-host-assets.mjs (the postinstall script cannot import TS). No test in the tree exercises the TS copy directly (grep over tests/ finds only a comment in postinstall.test.ts noting the mirroring), so behavioral drift between the two copies — e.g. one handling CRLF or empty frontmatter differently — would go undetected. Note the regex already fails to strip a zero-body frontmatter block ('---\n---\n'), a divergence-prone edge no test pins on either copy. Ideally the duplication itself is dissolved (share one .mjs source both can import); failing that, both copies need the same edge-case test matrix.
- Evidence: 4 items (top: "src/remediate/utils/hostAssets.ts:7-10 - the doc comment records the intentional duplicate in scripts/shared/install-host-assets.mjs.") — see audit-findings.json for the full list

### REL-7957ede5 — Job-level timeout is smaller than the sum of its own step timeouts

The gate and test jobs each declare timeout-minutes: 20 at the job level, but their own step-level timeout-minutes budgets sum to more (gate: 10+15=25; test: 10+10+15=35, plus checkout/setup overhead in both).

- Severity: low
- Confidence: medium
- Lens: reliability
- Grounding: grounded
- Files: `.github/workflows/publish-package.yml:50–51`, `.github/workflows/publish-package.yml:60–61`, `.github/workflows/publish-package.yml:66–67`, `.github/workflows/publish-package.yml:78–79` +2 more
- Details: The gate and test jobs each declare timeout-minutes: 20 at the job level, but their own step-level timeout-minutes budgets sum to more (gate: 10+15=25; test: 10+10+15=35, plus checkout/setup overhead in both). A run that legitimately uses most of a step's declared budget will instead be killed by the enclosing job timeout before that step's own timeout (and its 'trap ERR' diagnostic message) ever fires, so the failure surfaces as a generic runner timeout instead of the intended '::error::' attribution naming which step stalled.
- Evidence: 5 items (top: ".github/workflows/publish-package.yml:51 - `gate` job timeout-minutes: 20") — see audit-findings.json for the full list

### COR-d34d8956 — mergeGitignoreBlock leaves orphaned markers in place on degenerate marker states, causing duplicate managed blocks to accumulate

The replace-in-place path requires begin AND end markers with endIdx > beginIdx.

- Severity: low
- Confidence: medium
- Lens: correctness
- Grounding: grounded
- Files: `src/shared/io/gitignoreArtifacts.ts:177–195`
- Details: The replace-in-place path requires begin AND end markers with endIdx > beginIdx. If a .gitignore holds only one marker, or the END marker precedes BEGIN (e.g. a hand-edited or corrupted prior block), the function falls through to the append branch WITHOUT removing the stale marker lines. On the next run the stale marker pairs with the new block's marker (indexOf finds the first occurrence), so the 'replaced' span can clobber user lines between the orphaned marker and the new block, or the degenerate ordering persists and a fresh block is appended every run.
- Evidence: 4 items (top: "src/shared/io/gitignoreArtifacts.ts:178-182 - only the well-formed BEGIN-before-END case is replaced in place; all degenerate states (lone BEGIN, lone END, END-…") — see audit-findings.json for the full list

### COR-ebb4f359 — parseResetValue returns unparseable raw text as a reset timestamp

### DAT-fc991e7c — Schema regeneration writes each committed schema file in-place sequentially with no atomicity, so a mid-loop failure leaves the schema set half-updated

The generator overwrites each file under schemas/ one at a time directly at its final path.

- Severity: low
- Confidence: medium
- Lens: data_integrity
- Grounding: grounded
- Files: `scripts/audit/generate-schemas.mjs:20–28`
- Details: The generator overwrites each file under schemas/ one at a time directly at its final path. If rendering or writing a later file throws, earlier files are already replaced, leaving the committed generated-artifact set internally inconsistent (some schemas from the new zod source, some from the old). Git and the drift test bound the blast radius, but the write itself has no temp-then-rename or all-or-nothing step.
- Evidence: 2 items (top: "scripts/audit/generate-schemas.mjs:20-28 - each schema is rendered and written directly to its final path inside the loop; a failure on file N leaves files 1..N…") — see audit-findings.json for the full list

### TST-eee8da9d — Single-definition structural guard in staleness-convergence.test.ts uses a regex limited to `function` declarations, so a duplicate defined as an arrow function/const would not be detected

The 'only artifactStore.ts defines detectStaleArtifacts / DEPENDENCY_MAP / StalenessResult' test scans every .ts file under src/remediate with a regex anchored to `export\s+async\s+function\s+detectStaleArtifacts\b`.

- Confidence: medium
- Lens: tests
- Grounding: grounded

- Details: The 'only artifactStore.ts defines detectStaleArtifacts / DEPENDENCY_MAP / StalenessResult' test scans every .ts file under src/remediate with a regex anchored to `export\s+async\s+function\s+detectStaleArtifacts\b`. If a second, duplicate implementation were reintroduced using a different declaration style the test is explicitly meant to catch (e.g. `export const detectStaleArtifacts = async (...) => {...}`, a re-exported alias, or a static class method), the regex would not match it, `definers.detect` would still report length 1, and the guard would report false confidence that the staleness logic remains single-sourced.
- Evidence: 3 items (top: "tests/remediate/contract-pipeline-staleness-convergence.test.ts:136 - the guard only matches an `export async function detectStaleArtifacts` declaration form") — see audit-findings.json for the full list

- Details: Raised by the novelty perspective; the small-scale sibling of DR-007/DR-003. `runSlidingWindow` (src/shared/quota/slidingWindow.ts) is a bounded-concurrency runner functionally equivalent to p-limit/p-map; the EPERM/EBUSY/EACCES retry loop in src/shared/io/json.ts duplicates what p-retry provides declaratively; and the same exponential-backoff-with-deadline shape appears a third time in fileLock.ts. None is wrong individually — but the codebase now carries three slightly-different bespoke 'retry with backoff' implementations and one bespoke 'run N at a time' where p-limit + p-retry (tiny, zero-dep, same trust tier as commander/zod) would cover all four call sites with one widely-audited implementation. Establish one shared primitive either way — imported or owned — instead of accreting local variants.

### COR-7b0f806a — validateDispatchArtifacts records each result's plan phase but validates every referenced result against the implement-only contract

Either document results need their own validator selected by the stored phase, or the stored phase is dead data and 'document' should no longer validate as an acceptable phase.

The DerivedFrictionObservation.artifact doc claims a "(mixed)" label when several artifacts are aggregated, but the code never produces it — the area (or event id) is presented as if it were the artifact, and the derived note claims 'N repeat re-work' for work that never touched the same artifact twice.

### COR-90ce55c3 — Wall-stranded task claims stay held for the full 20-minute lease while the run is paused

When the host-path quota wall fires, prepareDispatchArtifacts releases the admission LEASES (reconcileAdmissionLeasesFromQuotaFile) but never clears the cooperative per-task CLAIMS it took via taskClaims.claimMany, even though nothing will be dispatched this turn.

- Severity: low
- Confidence: medium
- Lens: correctness
- Grounding: grounded
- Files: `src/audit/cli/dispatch.ts:449–460`, `src/audit/cli/dispatch.ts:755–786`
- Details: When the host-path quota wall fires, prepareDispatchArtifacts releases the admission LEASES (reconcileAdmissionLeasesFromQuotaFile) but never clears the cooperative per-task CLAIMS it took via taskClaims.claimMany, even though nothing will be dispatched this turn. The code explicitly releases claims for budget-deferred tasks because 'holding them would hoard deferred work a peer could otherwise pick up' — the identical rationale applies to wall-stranded emitted tasks (a peer IDE on a different quota pool is not walled), and re-grant is idempotent for the same runId so releasing would not break resume. Result: up to AUDIT_TASK_CLAIM_LEASE_MS (20 min) of peer-visible hoarding per wall pass.
- Evidence: 4 items (top: "src/audit/cli/dispatch.ts:452-459 - deferred-task claims are released with the anti-hoarding rationale in the adjacent comment.") — see audit-findings.json for the full list

### ARC-eaffc6ab — 'One user, no external consumers' design license sits alongside a publicly published npm package

Raised by the pragmatist perspective; kept as a lone but genuine framing observation (low confidence acknowledged).

- Severity: low
- Confidence: low
- Lens: architecture
- Grounding: grounded
- Systemic: yes
- Files: `package.json`, `CLAUDE.md`, `README.md`
- Details: Raised by the pragmatist perspective; kept as a lone but genuine framing observation (low confidence acknowledged). CLAUDE.md justifies unlimited complexity budget on 'one user, no external consumers,' while package.json publishes audit-tools publicly with discovery keywords and README onboards an unknown installer. If the honest audience is one operator, much of the everything-agnostic machinery (DR-006) is speculative; if it is the public npm audience the packaging implies, the 'complexity is free' license is the wrong governing assumption, because external users pay full price for internal-only conveniences (58-command CLI, 9-provider matrix, 24-obligation pipeline). The load-bearing assumption should be stated explicitly, since the two audiences justify materially different amounts of the ceremony flagged in DR-005/DR-006/DR-015.
- Evidence: runtime:flow:flow:host:npm-release-publish: not_confirmed — Deterministic runtime command failed with exit code 1: npm test

### TST-fc991e7c — worker-schema drift test is a sync guard over committed generated artifacts — the duplication it polices (committed schemas vs. zod sources) is the underlying cost

generate-schemas.mjs's own header documents that tests/audit/worker-schema-generation.test.mjs exists to fail whenever the committed schemas/ JSON drifts from what the zod sources would render.

- Severity: info
- Confidence: medium
- Lens: tests
- Grounding: grounded
- Files: `scripts/audit/generate-schemas.mjs:5–8`
- Details: generate-schemas.mjs's own header documents that tests/audit/worker-schema-generation.test.mjs exists to fail whenever the committed schemas/ JSON drifts from what the zod sources would render. That test is a drift guard: it keeps two representations of one contract (zod source and committed JSON schemas) in sync by assertion rather than by construction. Generating the schemas at build time into dist/ (they are already regenerable on demand) would remove the second copy and make the guard unnecessary; if committing generated JSON is a deliberate shipping requirement, the guard is the accepted workaround and this is informational.
- Evidence: 2 items (top: "scripts/audit/generate-schemas.mjs:5-8 - the header names the drift test and states its purpose is to detect committed-file drift from the zod sources, i.e. a s…") — see audit-findings.json for the full list
